## 4.3 First-Order Step Response (ASCII Schematic)

```
Output q_o(t)
   │                                               ┌───────────  KA (final value, 100%)
   │                                          ╱────┘
   │                                       ╱╱╱
   │                                    ╱╱╱
   │                                 ╱╱╱
   │                              ╱╱╱
   │                           ╱╱╱   ← 95% (t = 3τ)
   │                        ╱╱╱
   │                     ╱╱╱  ← 86.5% (t = 2τ)
   │                  ╱╱╱
   │               ╱╱╱  ← 63.2% (t = τ)
   │            ╱╱╱
   │         ╱╱╱
   │      ╱╱╱   ← 39.3% (t = 0.5τ)
   │   ╱╱╱

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each gridline intersection gives a useful exam-ready statistic. The curve never overshoots — first-order systems are inherently **non-oscillatory**.

---

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram of a Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[TemperatureInput\nHotBodyT2] --> nodeB[GlassBulb\nContainsLiquid]
    nodeB --> nodeC[LiquidExpansion\nVolumeVincreases]
    nodeC --> nodeD[CapillaryTube\nRiseOfColumnh]
    nodeD --> nodeE[CalibratedScale\nEngravedMarkings]
    nodeE --> nodeF[ObserverReadsValue\nIndicatedTemperatureT2]

    classDef hot fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00,color:#000
    classDef tube fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef scale fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    class nodeA hot
    class nodeB nodeC nodeD tube
    class nodeE nodeF scale
```

**Explanation:**
1. The bulb absorbs heat from the measured body.
2. The trapped liquid expands according to $\Delta V = V_0 \, \beta_{\text{app}} \, \Delta T$.
3. Since the glass also expands (much less), the *apparent* volume change drives the liquid up the capillary.
4. The rise $h$ is calibrated linearly against temperature assuming uniform capillary cross-section $A_c$:

$$h = \frac{V_0 \, \beta_{\text{app}}}{A_c} \, \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The bulb is the **primary sensing element**, the liquid is the **variable conversion element**, the capillary is the **variable manipulation** element, and the engraved scale is the **data presentation element**.

---

## 4.5 Comparative Block Diagram of Three Thermometer Families

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT[TemperatureSignal]
    nodeT --> nodeA[LiquidExpansionProperty]
    nodeT --> nodeB[DifferentialExpansionProperty]
    nodeT --> nodeC[ThermoelectricEMFProperty]

    nodeA --> nodeL[LiquidinGlassThermometer\nStaticMercuryAlcohol]
    nodeB --> nodeBi[BimetallicThermometer\nCoiledStripDial]
    nodeC --> nodeTC[Thermocouple\nHotJunctionEMF]

    nodeL --> nodeR1[OutputScaleorHeight]
    nodeBi --> nodeR2[OutputMechanicalDeflection]
    nodeTC --> nodeR3[OutputMillivoltsEMF]

    classDef sig fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#b8860b,color:#000
    classDef prop fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000
    class nodeT sig
    class nodeA nodeB nodeC prop
    class nodeL nodeBi nodeTC nodeR1 nodeR2 nodeR3 out
```

This block architecture shows the **unity of thermometric principle**: each thermometer is essentially a converter from a *temperature signal* to a *readable output*, differing only in the chosen thermometric property.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(a) **Static Sensitivity**
(b) **Hysteresis**
(c) **Dead Zone**

#### Model Answer
**(a) Static Sensitivity** — It is defined as the ratio of the change in output (response) to the corresponding change in input (stimulus) under steady-state conditions.

$$K = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$$

*Example:* For a linear thermocouple with $K = 40 \,\mu\text{V}/^\circ\text{C}$, a 25 °C rise in junction temperature produces $25 \times 40 = 1000 \,\mu\text{V}$ of EMF.

**(b) Hysteresis** — It is the maximum difference in output readings obtained when the input is first increased from a low value to a high value, and then decreased back, for the *same* input value.

*Example:* In a Bourdon pressure gauge, the same applied pressure gives a slightly different pointer reading depending on whether pressure was approached from below or above.

**(c) Dead Zone** — The range of input values over which there is no change in the output. Mathematically, the smallest change in input that produces a detectable change in output.

*Example:* A potentiometric displacement sensor with worn brushes may not register motion smaller than 0.1 mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking pattern (3 marks):** 1 mark for each correct definition, plus example. Strict key requires numerical/analytical example for full marks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain briefly:**
(a) **Difference between accuracy and precision**
(b) **What is meant by drift in an instrument?**
(c) **State the difference between a first-order and a second-order system.**

#### Model Answer
**(a)** *Accuracy* is closeness of a measurement to the **true value**; *precision* is closeness of repeated measurements to **each other**. A clock that is consistently 5 minutes fast is **precise but inaccurate**. A clock that sometimes reads 11:57, sometimes 12:03 is **inaccurate and imprecise**.

**(b)** *Drift* is the gradual change in the instrument's output with time, *even when the input and ambient conditions are held constant*. It is usually expressed as $\pm$ (units) per (time). Drift arises due to ageing of components, thermal cycling, contamination, or mechanical wear.

**(c)** A **first-order system** is described by a first-order differential equation; its step response is a single exponential rise with no overshoot. A **second-order system** requires a second-order ODE and may exhibit overshoot and oscillation depending on the damping ratio $\zeta$.

| Feature | 1st Order | 2nd Order |
|---|---|---|
| Equation | $\tau \dot{y} + y = K x$ | $\ddot{y} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{y} + \omega_n^2 y = K\omega_n^2 x$ |
| Overshoot | None | Yes (if $\zeta < 1$) |
| Step response | Pure exponential | Exponential $\times$ sinusoid (underdamped) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**(a)** Explain in detail the **static characteristics** of a measuring instrument. Define accuracy, precision, sensitivity, resolution, linearity, hysteresis, and drift with appropriate formulas.

**(b)** A pressure gauge reads 12.4 bar when the actual pressure is 12.0 bar. The gauge has a full-scale range of 0–20 bar. Determine:
1. Absolute error
2. Percentage relative error
3. Percentage accuracy based on full scale
4. Percentage accuracy based on span

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Static characteristics** describe the performance of an instrument when the input is essentially constant (steady state) or changing slowly enough for transients to die out. The principal static characteristics are:

1. **Accuracy** — closeness of measured value to true value. Expressed as $\pm$% of full-scale or $\pm$% of reading.

$$A = \frac{|M - T|}{T} \times 100\% \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{|M - T|}{FS} \times 100\%$$

2. **Precision** — degree of reproducibility (scatter) of repeated measurements. Quantified by standard deviation $\sigma$.

3. **Sensitivity ($K$)** — ratio of output change to input change.

$$K = \frac{\Delta q_o}{\Delta q_i}$$

4. **Resolution ($R$)** — smallest measurable input increment.

5. **Linearity** — conformity of calibration curve to a straight line. Measured as maximum deviation from best-fit line, expressed as % of FS.

6. **Hysteresis** — difference in output for same input depending on direction of approach.

7. **Drift** — slow, unwanted change of output with time.

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $M = 12.4$ bar, $T = 12.0$ bar, FS = 20 bar, Span = 20 bar (since 0 to 20).

**(1) Absolute error:**
$$\varepsilon = M - T = 12.4 - 12.0 = +0.4 \text{ bar}$$

**(2) Percentage relative error:**
$$\varepsilon_{\text{rel}} = \frac{\varepsilon}{T} \times 100 = \frac{0.4}{12.0} \times 100 = 3.333\%$$

**(3) Percentage accuracy (% FS):**
$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{FS - \vert \varepsilon \vert}{FS} \times 100 = \frac{20 - 0.4}{20} \times 100 = 98.0\%$$

**(4) Percentage accuracy based on span:**
Since span = FS = 20 bar here, this gives the same result: **98.0%**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> - Confusing **accuracy based on reading** vs **accuracy based on full scale** — these give *different* numerical answers when the reading is not at FS. KTU wants you to specify which one you are computing.
> - Forgetting to include the **absolute value** when reporting accuracy.
> - Using the wrong denominator (some students divide by $M$ instead of $T$).

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**(a)** Explain the **dynamic characteristics** of an instrument with reference to a first-order system. Derive the step response and define time constant, rise time, and settling time.

**(b)** A temperature sensor behaves as a first-order system with time constant $\tau = 5$ s and static sensitivity $K = 0.1 \text{ V}/^\circ\text{C}$. The sensor is placed in a bath whose temperature suddenly changes from 25 °C to 75 °C at $t = 0$. Determine:
1. The indicated temperature at $t = 5$ s.
2. The time required for the sensor output to reach 95 % of its final value.
3. The bandwidth of the sensor in Hz.

#### Model Solution for (a)

A **first-order system** is governed by:

$$\tau \frac{dq_o}{dt} + q_o = K q_i$$

For a step input $q_i(t) = A$ (constant for $t \geq 0$), the solution is:

$$q_o(t) = KA\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$$

Key dynamic parameters:

- **Time constant $\tau$:** Time taken for output to reach **63.2 %** of its final value. Lower $\tau$ = faster instrument.
- **Rise time $t_r$:** Time for output to go from **10 % to 90 %** of final value. For first order: $t_r \approx 2.2 \tau$.
- **Settling time $t_s$:** Time for output to remain within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of final value. $t_s \approx 4\tau$ (2%) or $t_s \approx 3\tau$ (5%).

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $\tau = 5$ s, $K = 0.1$ V/°C, $\Delta T = 75 - 25 = 50$ °C.

**(1) Output at $t = 5$ s:**

The output voltage at $t = 5$ s:

$$q_o(5) = K \cdot \Delta T \cdot (1 - e^{-5/5}) = 0.1 \times 50 \times (1 - e^{-1})$$

$$= 5 \times (1 - 0.3679) = 5 \times 0.6321 = 3.160 \text{ V}$$

Equivalent indicated temperature:

$$T_{\text{ind}} = 25 + \frac{q_o}{K} = 25 + \frac{3.160}{0.1} = 25 + 31.6 = 56.6\,^\circ\text{C}$$

**(2) Time to reach 95 % of final value:**

$$0.95 = 1 - e^{-t/\tau}$$

$$e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05$$

$$\frac{t}{\tau} = -\ln(0.05) = 2.996$$

$$t = 2.996 \times 5 = 14.98 \approx 15 \text{ s}$$

**(3) Bandwidth:**

$$\omega_{BW} = \frac{1}{\tau} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$f_{BW} = \frac{\omega_{BW}}{2\pi} = \frac{0.2}{6.283} \approx 0.0318 \text{ Hz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Dynamic response traps:**
> - Many students write the response as $q_o = K \Delta T \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$ (missing the "1 − " term). This gives the *transient deviation from final value*, not the actual output.
> - Some confuse the **time to reach 95%** with the **rise time**. They are different concepts; rise time is 10%–90%, while the 95% time is roughly $3\tau$.
> - Forgetting to convert between $\omega$ (rad/s) and $f$ (Hz) when reporting bandwidth.

---

## 5.3 Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer — Focused Problem

### Question [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
A mercury-in-glass thermometer has a bulb volume $V_0 = 0.5 \text{ cm}^3$ at 0 °C and a capillary cross-sectional area $A_c = 0.02 \text{ mm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^2$. Given:
- Cubical expansion of mercury: $\beta = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} \; /^\circ\text{C}$
- Linear expansion of glass: $\alpha = 8.5 \times 10^{-6} \; /^\circ\text{C}$

Find:
1. The **apparent expansion coefficient** of mercury in glass.
2. The **rise of mercury column** for a temperature increase of 100 °C.
3. The **length of capillary** required if the scale is to read up to 200 °C.

#### Model Solution

**(1) Apparent expansion coefficient:**

$$\beta_{\text{app}} = \beta - 3\alpha = 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 3 \times 8.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$= 1.82 \times 10^{-4} - 0.255 \times 10^{-4} = 1.565 \times 10^{-4} \; /^\circ\text{C}$$

**(2) Rise in column for $\Delta T = 100$ °C:**

Apparent volume increase:

$$\Delta V = V_0 \, \beta_{\text{app}} \, \Delta T = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.565 \times 10^{-4} \times 100$$

$$= 7.825 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

Rise in column:

$$h = \frac{\Delta V}{A_c} = \frac{7.825 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.391 \text{ m} = 39.1 \text{ cm}$$

**(3) Length required for 200 °C scale:**

$$\Delta V_{200} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.565 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 1.565 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3$$

$$L = \frac{1.565 \times 10^{-8}}{2 \times 10^{-8}} = 0.7825 \text{ m} \approx 78.3 \text{ cm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall — Unit conversion:** Students frequently mix cm³, mm², m³, and m². The cross-sectional area was given in mm² but must be converted to m² to match SI units. A wrong unit conversion can give a column length that is off by a factor of 10⁶.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4**

### Static Characteristics
- **Accuracy** = closeness to true value; expressed as %FS or %reading.
- **Precision** = reproducibility; measured by standard deviation $\sigma$.
- **Sensitivity** = slope of calibration curve $K = \Delta q_o / \Delta q_i$.
- **Resolution** = smallest measurable input increment; for $n$-bit digital, $R = S/2^n$.
- **Range/Span** = upper range value − lower range value.
- **Linearity** = max deviation from best-fit line, as %FS.
- **Hysteresis** = output depends on direction of input change; max difference expressed as %span.
- **Drift** = unwanted output change with time; zero drift and span drift distinguished.
- **Dead Zone** = input range that produces no output change.
- **Static Error** = $M - T$; relative error = error / true value.

### Dynamic Characteristics
- Most temperature/thermal instruments are **first-order** with time constant $\tau$.
- Step response: $q_o(t) = KA(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$.
- Memorize key percentages: $1\tau = 63.2\%$, $2\tau = 86.5\%$, $3\tau = 95\%$, $4\tau = 98.2\%$, $5\tau = 99.3\%$.
- Rise time (10%→90%): $t_r \approx 2.2\tau$.
- Settling time (2%): $t_s \approx 4\tau$.
- Magnitude ratio: $M(\omega) = 1/\sqrt{1+(\omega\tau)^2}$.
- Phase lag: $\phi(\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega\tau)$.
- Bandwidth: $\omega_{BW} = 1/\tau$; $f_{BW} = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- **Dead time** is a pure transportation delay (no response at all initially).

### Temperature Measurement Fundamentals
- Temperature is **NOT directly measurable** — must infer from secondary thermometric properties.
- **Thermodynamic scale** is absolute; **IPTS-90 / ITS-90** is the practical reference.

### Liquid-in-Glass Thermometer (Key)
- Working principle: apparent volume expansion of liquid in a glass container.
- Apparent expansion: $\beta_{\text{app}} = \beta_{\text{liquid}} - 3\alpha_{\text{glass}}$.
- Column rise: $h = V_0 \, \beta_{\text{app}} \, \Delta T / A_c$.
- Mercury range: −39 °C to 357 °C; alcohol (colored) for lower ranges.
- Sources of error: emergent stem correction, parallax, calibration drift, bulb ageing.

### Three Types of Thermometers (at a glance)
| Type | Principle | Output | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Liquid-in-Glass | Volume expansion | Column height | −200 to 600 °C |
| Bimetallic | Differential linear expansion | Mechanical deflection (pointer) | −50 to 500 °C |
| Thermocouple | Seebeck EMF | Millivolts | −270 to 2300 °C |

### KTU Board Exam Survival Tips
- Always state **units** in your final answer.
- For step-response problems, write the **general solution first**, then plug in numerical values — this earns method marks even if arithmetic slips.
- Use `=` aligned structures; avoid mixing decimal approximations with symbolic expressions.
- For dynamic characteristics problems, **draw the step response curve** showing 63.2% and 95% points — it scores easy marks.
- For thermometer problems, **always convert all areas and volumes to the same unit system** before computing.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Filled-System (Vapour Pressure & Gas) Thermometers

### Vapour Pressure Thermometer
A **Vapour Pressure Thermometer** is a filled-system thermal expansion type temperature measuring instrument that operates on the principle that the saturation vapour pressure of a volatile liquid is a unique, single-valued function of its temperature. The instrument consists of a sensing bulb containing a volatile liquid (partially filled), a capillary tube, and a pressure-measuring element (Bourdon tube) connected to an indicator.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The vapour pressure thermometer is a *class A* filled-system thermometer where the relationship between temperature and pressure is **non-linear** because vapour pressure does not vary linearly with temperature. It gives good accuracy over a **narrow range** and is highly sensitive at the upper end of the range.

**Common Fill Fluids & Their Ranges:**
- **Methyl Chloride:** -40 °C to 200 °C
- **Butane:** -20 °C to 100 °C
- **Propane:** -50 °C to 100 °C
- **Carbon Disulphide:** 30 °C to 200 °C
- **Water (distilled):** 100 °C to 300 °C
- **Mercury:** 250 °C to 500 °C

### Gas Thermometer (Constant Volume Type)
A **Gas Thermometer** is a filled-system thermometer using an inert gas (typically **Nitrogen** or **Helium**) at high pressure. It relies on the **Gay-Lussac's Law** (constant volume gas law): for a fixed mass of gas at constant volume, the absolute pressure is directly proportional to the absolute temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> The relationship is essentially **linear** when pressure is plotted against absolute temperature (Kelvin). This linearity is a major advantage of gas thermometers over vapour pressure systems.

### Conceptual Analogy: Filled-System Thermometers
Think of a **soda bottle with a balloon on top**:
- **Vapour pressure type** → Only a small amount of liquid at the bottom; as you heat it, the liquid evaporates faster and the vapour pushes the balloon more (non-linearly).
- **Gas type** → The bottle is completely filled with gas (no liquid); heating makes the gas molecules move faster, pushing the balloon more (linearly with temperature).

---

## 1.2 Bimetallic Thermometer

A **Bimetallic Thermometer** uses the differential thermal expansion of two metals bonded together. It consists of two strips of metals with **different coefficients of linear expansion** ($\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$) welded or riveted along their entire length. When temperature changes, the strip bends toward the metal with the **lower** expansion coefficient.

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Active Metal:** The metal with the **higher** coefficient of expansion (e.g., **Invar**, **Brass**, **Manganese-Nickel-Copper alloy**, or **Chromel**). It expands more on heating.
>
> **Passive Metal (Low Expansion Side):** The metal with the **lower** coefficient of expansion (e.g., **Invar** – an alloy of 36% Ni + 64% Fe with $\alpha \approx 1 \times 10^{-6}$/°C). It expands less.

**Common Bimetallic Combinations:**
| Active (High α) | Passive (Low α) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Brass | Invar | Industrial dial thermometers |
| Manganese-Nickel-Copper | Invar | Aerospace & cryogenics |
| Chromel | Invar | High-temperature gauges |

**Configurations:** Helical coil (most common in dial thermometers), spiral, cantilever beam, and straight strip.

### Conceptual Analogy: Bimetallic Strip
Imagine **two friends of different heights** walking while holding hands. If both grow at the same rate, they remain side by side. But if one grows *faster* than the other, the pair will **curve** toward the shorter one. The bimetallic strip works the same way — heat causes one metal to "grow" (expand) more than the other, creating mechanical bending used to drive a pointer.

---

## 1.3 Resistance Thermometer (RTD)

A **Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)** is a temperature sensor that exploits the principle that the electrical resistance of pure metals **increases** with temperature in a predictable, repeatable manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Definition:** *An RTD is a passive resistive transducer in which the resistance $R_t$ of a metallic sensing element is a function of the measured temperature $T$. The relationship for platinum (the most common RTD material) is given by the **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation**.*

**Standard Materials for RTD:**
- **Platinum (Pt)** – Most popular, range: **−200 °C to 850 °C**, highest stability & linearity, used as **ITS-90 standard** between −259 °C to 962 °C.
- **Copper (Cu)** – Range: −50 °C to 150 °C, very linear but low resistivity.
- **Nickel (Ni)** – Range: −80 °C to 320 °C, higher sensitivity but less linear.
- **Tungsten, Iron, Balco (Ni-Fe)** – Special-purpose RTDs.

> [!NOTE]
> The **Resistance Temperature Coefficient (α)** for platinum is standardized at **0.00385 Ω/Ω/°C** (European curve, IEC 60751) or **0.00392 Ω/Ω/°C** (American curve).

### Conceptual Analogy: RTD
Imagine a **garden hose with a marble stuck inside**. As water (electrons) flows through, the marble resists the flow. If you heat the hose, the marble jiggles more (atomic vibrations increase) and partially blocks the path more — making it **harder** for water to pass. This is exactly what happens in an RTD: **higher temperature → more atomic vibration → more resistance to electron flow → higher resistance**.

---

## 1.4 Principles of Thermoelectricity

### The Seebeck Effect
**Thomas Johann Seebeck** discovered in **1821** that when two dissimilar metals are joined to form a closed loop, and the two junctions are maintained at **different temperatures**, an **electromotive force (EMF)** is generated which causes a current to flow in the loop. This phenomenon is called the **Seebeck Effect**.

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Seebeck Effect (KTU Standard Definition):** *"The generation of an electromotive force (EMF) in a closed circuit consisting of two dissimilar conductors (or semiconductors) when the junctions are maintained at different temperatures."*

The net EMF generated is given by:
$$E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_A(T) - S_B(T))\, dT$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** of materials A and B (in V/°C or µV/°C), $T_1$ is the cold-junction (reference) temperature, and $T_2$ is the hot-junction (measuring) temperature.

**The two junctions are called:**
- **Hot Junction (Measuring Junction):** Exposed to the unknown temperature $T_2$ (also called *measuring junction*).
- **Cold Junction (Reference Junction):** Maintained at a known reference temperature $T_1$ (usually 0 °C in ice-bath calibration).

### Conceptual Analogy: Seebeck Effect
Think of a **two-person tug-of-war**, where each person pulls electrons with a *different* strength (Seebeck coefficient). When both ends are at the same temperature, both pull equally, and no current flows. But when you **heat one end** (hot junction), that side's atoms get more energetic and "kick" electrons toward the cooler side, producing a measurable voltage. This voltage is the **Seebeck EMF**.

---

## 1.5 Module 4 At-a-Glance Summary Table

| Instrument | Principle | Output / Measured Variable | Linearity | Range (Typical) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Vapour Pressure Thermometer | Saturation vapour pressure vs T | Pressure → Displacement | Non-linear | −50 °C to 300 °C |
| Gas Thermometer (Const. V) | Gay-Lussac's law ($P \propto T$) | Pressure → Displacement | Linear (in K) | −270 °C to 1100 °C |
| Bimetallic Thermometer | Differential thermal expansion | Bending → Displacement | Non-linear | −50 °C to 600 °C |
| Resistance Thermometer (RTD) | $R_t$ vs $T$ of metal | Resistance → Voltage | Nearly linear | −200 °C to 850 °C |
| Thermocouple (Seebeck) | Thermoelectric EMF | EMF (mV) | Slightly non-linear | −270 °C to 2300 °C |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Vapour Pressure Thermometer — Theoretical Breakdown

### Operating Principle
A volatile liquid partially fills a sealed **bulb**, connected via a capillary tube to a **Bourdon tube** pressure gauge. Above the liquid, the space contains only the saturated vapour of the liquid. The **interface between liquid and vapour** is the active element.

**Key Operating Logic (in steps):**
1. The bulb is exposed to the process temperature.
2. Heat transfers to the volatile liquid, raising its temperature.
3. A new thermodynamic equilibrium is established between the liquid and its vapour.
4. The **saturation vapour pressure** uniquely determines the new temperature.
5. Vapour pressure is transmitted via the capillary to the Bourdon tube.
6. The Bourdon tube deflects proportionally to the pressure, driving an indicator pointer.

### The Clausius–Clapeyron Equation
The vapour pressure as a function of temperature is governed by:

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{L}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

where $L$ = latent heat of vaporization (J/mol), $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** (universal gas constant), and $T$ is in Kelvin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $L$ varies slightly with temperature, the relationship is **not exactly exponential**, which is why the calibration curve of a vapour pressure thermometer is non-linear and requires individual calibration.

### Advantages & Limitations
**Advantages:**
- Simple, rugged, low cost.
- Higher sensitivity than gas or liquid thermometers near the boiling point of the fill fluid.
- Remote indication possible (capillary length up to 30 m).

**Limitations:**
- Non-linear scale (compressed at lower temperatures).
- Affected by ambient temperature along the capillary (requires **inert gas** compensation or full-bore capillary design).
- Limited range per fill fluid.

---

## 2.2 Gas Thermometer (Constant Volume) — Theoretical Breakdown

### Operating Principle
The bulb is filled with an inert gas (nitrogen or helium) at high pressure (typically 1–3 MPa). The **capillary and Bourdon tube are completely filled** with the same gas. The Bourdon tube measures pressure, and from pressure, temperature is inferred.

### Governing Law: Gay-Lussac's Law (Charles's Law at constant V)
For a fixed mass of gas at constant volume:

$$\frac{P_1}{T_1} = \frac{P_2}{T_2} = \text{constant}$$

Therefore:

$$P = k \cdot T \quad \text{(linear in absolute temperature)}$$

or

$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

In terms of Celsius:

$$t = \frac{P - P_0}{P_0} \cdot \frac{1}{\alpha} = \frac{P - P_0}{P_0} \cdot 273.15$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Gas Correction (Van der Waals):** For high-precision work, especially with Helium, the real-gas behavior is described by the van der Waals equation:
> $$\left(P + \frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V - b) = nRT$$
> where $a$ accounts for intermolecular attraction and $b$ for molecular volume.

### Advantages
- **Linear** relationship between pressure and absolute temperature.
- Wide operating range.
- Independent of fill fluid properties (any inert gas works).
- **High accuracy** — used as a primary standard in metrology labs.

### Limitations
- Requires **bulky** pressure-measuring device.
- Bulb volume is large — slow response.
- The pressure can be very high (safety concerns).

---

## 2.3 Bimetallic Thermometer — Theoretical Analysis

### Bending Mechanics
For a bimetallic strip of length $L$, with thicknesses $t_1$ and $t_2$, bonded together, the radius of curvature $R$ after a temperature change $\Delta T$ is given by:

$$\frac{1}{R} = \frac{6(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)(T_2 - T_1)(1 + m)^2}{h \left[3(1+m)^2 + (1+mn)\left(m^2 + \frac{1}{mn}\right)\right]}$$

where $m = t_1/t_2$, $n = E_1/E_2$, $h = t_1 + t_2$, and $E$ = Young's modulus.

**Simplified deflection form** (for equal thicknesses and ideal case):

$$\delta \approx \frac{L^2 \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{2t}$$

where $\delta$ = tip deflection, $L$ = free length, $t$ = total thickness.

### The Three KTU-Relevant Helical Coil Equations
When the strip is wound into a helix of mean radius $r_m$ and $N$ turns:

**Angular deflection** $\theta$ (in radians):

$$\theta = \frac{360 \cdot L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{\pi \cdot t}$$

**Linear tip movement** of the helix axis:

$$\Delta x = \frac{\pi \cdot r_m \cdot L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

**Active length required** for desired angular deflection:

$$L = \frac{\pi \cdot t \cdot \theta}{360 \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}$$

> [!TIP]
> For a KTU 14-mark question, if a problem gives $(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)$, $L$, $t$, and $\Delta T$, the expected answer is the angular deflection $\theta$ or the linear movement $\Delta x$.

### Industrial Forms & Applications
- **Helical coil** in dial thermometers (most common).
- **Spiral** in recording instruments.
- **Cantilever strip** in thermostats & over-temperature switches.
- **Axial rod-and-tube** in thermostatic radiator valves.

---

## 2.4 Resistance Thermometer (RTD) — Detailed Theory

### The Fundamental Equations

**Linear Approximation (for small ranges):**

$$R_t = R_0(1 + \alpha t)$$

where $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C, $R_t$ = resistance at $t$ °C, $\alpha$ = temperature coefficient of resistance.

**Exact Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (for Platinum, −200 °C to 0 °C):**

$$R_t = R_0 \left[1 + At + Bt^2 + C(t - 100)\,t^3\right]$$

**For 0 °C to 850 °C:**

$$R_t = R_0(1 + At + Bt^2)$$

where for Pt-100 (IEC 60751):
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ /°C²
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ /°C⁴ (only for $t < 0$)

### Sensitivity

$$\frac{dR_t}{dt} = R_0 \cdot \alpha$$

For Pt-100, $\alpha = 0.00385$ /°C, so sensitivity ≈ **0.385 Ω/°C**.

### Wheatstone Bridge Configuration
A typical RTD is connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** to convert resistance to voltage:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \cdot \frac{R_t R_3 - R_2 R_4}{(R_t + R_2)(R_3 + R_4)}$$

For balanced bridge ($V_{\text{out}} = 0$): $R_t = \dfrac{R_2 R_4}{R_3}$

### Lead-Wire Compensation
Because RTD is low resistance (100 Ω nominal), lead resistance cannot be ignored. **Three-wire** and **four-wire (Kelvin)** connections are used to eliminate lead-resistance errors.

---

## 2.5 Seebeck Effect & Thermocouple Theory

### The Three Thermoelectric Effects
> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Seebeck Effect is **one of three** thermoelectric effects. The KTU examiner expects you to know all three.

| Effect | Discovered By | Year | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Seebeck Effect** | T.J. Seebeck | 1821 | Temperature difference → EMF |
| **Peltier Effect** | J.C.A. Peltier | 1834 | Current flow → Heating/Cooling at junction |
| **Thomson Effect** | William Thomson (Lord Kelvin) | 1856 | Current + temperature gradient → Heat in single conductor |

### The Seebeck Coefficient
The Seebeck coefficient (thermoelectric power) of a material:

$$S = \lim_{\Delta T \to 0} \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta T} \quad \text{[V/K or µV/K]}$$

The **net Seebeck coefficient** of a thermocouple (A vs B):

$$S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$$

### Three Fundamental Laws of Thermoelectric Circuits (KTU Hot Topic)

**Law 1 — Law of Intermediate Metals:**
*Inserting a third (intermediate) metal into a thermocouple circuit does **not** change the net EMF, provided both new junctions are at the **same temperature**.*

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AC}(T_1, T_2) + E_{CB}(T_1, T_2)$$

**Law 2 — Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**
*The EMF generated with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_3$ equals the sum of EMFs with junctions at $T_1, T_2$ and $T_2, T_3$ (all with the same pair of metals).*

$$E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$$

**Law 3 — Law of Sum and Difference:**
*If two thermocouples (A-B and C-D) generate EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$, then the EMF of a circuit combining A with C and B with D (with appropriate polarity) is $E = E_1 \pm E_2$.*

### Standard Thermocouple Types (ITS-90)

| Type | Materials | Range (°C) | Sensitivity (µV/°C) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **B** | Pt-30%Rh / Pt-6%Rh | 0 to 1820 | ~10 | High-temperature furnaces |
| **E** | Chromel / Constantan | −270 to 1000 | ~68 | Highest sensitivity, oxidizing |
| **J** | Iron / Constantan | −210 to 1200 | ~55 | Reducing atmospheres |
| **K** | Chromel / Alumel | −270 to 1370 | ~41 | Most common industrial TC |
| **R** | Pt-13%Rh / Pt | −50 to 1768 | ~10 | Laboratory standard |
| **S** | Pt-10%Rh / Pt | −50 to 1768 | ~10 | Laboratory standard |
| **T** | Copper / Constantan | −270 to 400 | ~46 | Cryogenic & food industry |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Topic | Formula | Description / Key Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vapour Pressure (Clausius–Clapeyron) | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\frac{L}{R}(1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ | $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 2 | Gas Thermometer (Const V) | $P_2/P_1 = T_2/T_1$ | Linear in Kelvin |
| 3 | Gas Thermometer (Celsius) | $t = (P - P_0)/(P_0 \alpha)$, $\alpha = 1/273.15$ | Use $T = t + 273.15$ |
| 4 | Bimetallic Tip Deflection | $\delta = L^2(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (2t)$ | Cantilever approximation |
| 5 | Bimetallic Angular Deflection | $\theta = 360 L (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2)\Delta T / (\pi t)$ | Degrees |
| 6 | RTD Linear | $R_t = R_0(1 + \alpha t)$ | Small-range approximation |
| 7 | RTD Callendar–Van Dusen (0–850 °C) | $R_t = R_0(1 + At + Bt^2)$ | $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$ |
| 8 | RTD Callendar–Van Dusen (below 0) | $R_t = R_0[1 + At + Bt^2 + C(t-100)t^3]$ | $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$ |
| 9 | RTD Sensitivity | $dR_t/dt = R_0 \alpha$ | For Pt-100 ≈ 0.385 Ω/°C |
| 10 | Seebeck EMF | $E = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (S_A - S_B)\, dT$ | µV range |
| 11 | Net Seebeck Coefficient | $S_{AB} = S_A - S_B$ | Material property |
| 12 | Intermediate Metal Law | $E_{AB} = E_{AC} + E_{CB}$ | All at same $T_1$, $T_2$ |
| 13 | Intermediate Temp Law | $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$ | Same TC pair |
| 14 | Cold-Junction Compensation | $E_{\text{actual}}(T, T_0) = E_{\text{meas}}(T, T_{\text{ref}}) + E_{\text{generated}}(T_{\text{ref}}, T_0)$ | Where $T_0 = 0$ °C |
| 15 | Wheatstone Bridge Output | $V_o = V_{ex}(R_tR_3 - R_2R_4)/[(R_t+R_2)(R_3+R_4)]$ | For RTD |
| 16 | Thermocouple Sensitivity (K-type) | $S_K \approx 41\ \mu\text{V/°C}$ at 100 °C | Approximate |

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exam, **Formula Sheet items 2, 4, 6, 7, 10, 12, 13, 14** are the most frequently asked.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Vapour Pressure** → Refrigeration pressure gauges, cryogenic storage level measurement (when bulb senses gas above liquid).
- **Gas Thermometers** → National metrology institutes (PTB, NIST) as primary temperature standards.
- **Bimetallic** → Room thermostats, oven controls, automotive engine coolant gauges, fire-sprinkler heads.
- **RTD** → Industrial process control (chemical reactors), HVAC, pharmaceutical sterilization (Pt-100), food & beverage.
- **Thermocouples** → Gas turbine exhaust, furnace profiling, molten-metal measurement, kilns, exhaust gas after-treatment.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Gas Thermometer Temperature from Pressure Data

**Problem:** A constant-volume gas thermometer uses nitrogen at initial pressure $P_0 = 100$ kPa at 0 °C. When placed in a bath, the new pressure is $P_t = 136.5$ kPa. Find the bath temperature.

**Step 1: Convert 0 °C to absolute temperature.**
$$T_0 = 0 + 273.15 = 273.15\ \text{K}$$

**Step 2: Apply Gay-Lussac's Law at constant volume.**
$$\frac{P_0}{T_0} = \frac{P_t}{T_t}$$

**Step 3: Solve for $T_t$ in Kelvin.**
$$T_t = T_0 \cdot \frac{P_t}{P_0}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values.**
$$T_t = 273.15 \cdot \frac{136.5}{100}$$

**Step 5: Compute the result.**
$$T_t = 273.15 \times 1.365 = 372.85\ \text{K}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Celsius.**
$$t = T_t - 273.15 = 372.85 - 273.15 = 99.7 \approx 100\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** This is a neat result! With ~36.5% increase in absolute pressure, the bath is essentially at the **boiling point of water**, confirming the standard 100 °C steam point. This is a common KTU short-answer question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bimetallic Strip Deflection (Helical Coil)

**Problem:** A helical bimetallic coil has $L = 200$ mm active length, total thickness $t = 0.5$ mm, made of brass ($\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6}/°C$) and Invar ($\alpha_2 = 1 \times 10^{-6}/°C$). Find the angular deflection for $\Delta T = 100\ °C$ and the tip linear movement for $r_m = 8$ mm.

**Step 1: Compute differential expansion coefficient.**
$$\alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1) \times 10^{-6} = 18 \times 10^{-6}\ /^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Use the angular deflection formula.**
$$\theta = \frac{360 \cdot L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{\pi \cdot t}$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**
$$\theta = \frac{360 \times (0.2) \times (18 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100}{\pi \times (0.5 \times 10^{-3})}$$

**Step 4: Compute numerator.**
$$360 \times 0.2 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 0.1296$$

**Step 5: Compute denominator.**
$$\pi \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 6: Divide.**
$$\theta = \frac{0.1296}{1.5708 \times 10^{-3}} = 82.51\ \text{degrees}$$

**Step 7: Convert to radians.**
$$\theta_{\text{rad}} = 82.51 \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 1.440\ \text{rad}$$

**Step 8: Compute the linear tip movement.**
$$\Delta x = \frac{\pi \cdot r_m \cdot L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

$$\Delta x = \frac{\pi \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.2 \times (18 \times 10^{-6}) \times 100}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 9: Simplify the expression.**

$$\Delta x = \frac{3.1416 \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 100}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 10: Compute step by step.**

First, combine $L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T$:
$$0.2 \times 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 = 3.6 \times 10^{-4}$$

Then multiply by $\pi \cdot r_m$:
$$\pi \times 8 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.6 \times 10^{-4} = 3.1416 \times 28.8 \times 10^{-7} = 9.048 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 11: Divide by $t = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$.**
$$\Delta x = \frac{9.048 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.8096 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m} = 18.1\ \text{mm}$$

> [!TIP]
> This $\Delta x = 18.1$ mm is sufficient to drive a pointer in a 100 mm diameter industrial dial thermometer through a suitable mechanical linkage.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: RTD Temperature Using Callendar-Van Dusen Equation

**Problem:** A Pt-100 RTD has $R_0 = 100.00\ \Omega$ at 0 °C. Find the resistance at $t = 200\ °C$ using the Callendar-Van Dusen equation.

**Step 1: Use the 0–850 °C form.**
$$R_t = R_0(1 + At + Bt^2)$$

**Step 2: Substitute constants.**
$$A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}\ /^\circ\text{C},\ \ B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}\ /(^\circ\text{C})^2$$

**Step 3: Compute $At$.**
$$At = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 = 0.78166$$

**Step 4: Compute $Bt^2$.**
$$Bt^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 200^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 40000 = -0.0231$$

**Step 5: Sum the bracketed terms.**
$$1 + At + Bt^2 = 1 + 0.78166 - 0.0231 = 1.75856$$

**Step 6: Multiply by $R_0$.**
$$R_t = 100 \times 1.75856 = 175.856\ \Omega$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compare with the linear approximation: $R_t = 100(1 + 0.00385 \times 200) = 100(1.77) = 177.0\ \Omega$. The quadratic correction reduces it by **~1.14 Ω**, which is significant for high-precision calibration.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Seebeck EMF (K-Type Thermocouple)

**Problem:** A K-type (Chromel–Alumel) thermocouple has approximate sensitivity $S_{AB} = 41\ \mu\text{V}/°C$ (assumed constant over the range). Cold junction is at 25 °C, hot junction at 350 °C. Find (a) the EMF, and (b) the equivalent cold-junction-referenced EMF at 0 °C.

**Part (a): Direct EMF**

**Step 1: Temperature difference.**
$$\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}} = 350 - 25 = 325\ ^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Apply Seebeck's linearized form.**
$$E = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**
$$E = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times 325 = 13.325 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{V} = 13.325\ \text{mV}$$

**Part (b): Cold-Junction Compensation (Law of Intermediate Temperatures)**

**Step 4: Equivalent EMF at 0 °C reference.**
$$E(T_0 = 0, T_2 = 350) = E(0, 25) + E(25, 350)$$

**Step 5: Compute $E(0, 25)$.**
$$E(0, 25) = 41 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 = 1.025\ \text{mV}$$

**Step 6: Sum.**
$$E(0, 350) = 1.025 + 13.325 = 14.35\ \text{mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This shows why **cold-junction compensation (CJC)** is critical. Without it, a chart lookup at 13.325 mV (K-type) gives ~325 °C, not 350 °C — an error of **25 °C**!

---

## 3.5 Derivation: EMF Using Intermediate Metal Law (Practical Application)

**Problem:** A copper-constantan (T-type) thermocouple measures a hot junction at 200 °C. A copper lead wire is connected to the cold junction (25 °C) to bring the signal to a measurement device. Show that the intermediate copper wire does not affect the EMF.

**Step 1: Initial EMF without lead wire.**
$$E_{\text{Cu-Con}}(25, 200) = ?$$

**Step 2: With copper lead inserted at cold junction (both ends at 25 °C).**
By **Law of Intermediate Metals**:
$$E_{\text{Cu-Con}}(25, 200) = E_{\text{Cu-A}}(25, 200) + E_{\text{A-Con}}(25, 200)$$

**Step 3: Physical interpretation.**
The two extra junctions formed (Cu-A and A-Con) are both at 25 °C, so they generate **zero** net EMF. Thus the total EMF remains:
$$E_{\text{total}} = E_{\text{Cu-Con}}(25, 200)$$

> [!TIP]
> This is why copper lead wires can be safely connected to a T-type thermocouple cold junction — the intermediate metal law guarantees no error.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU 14-Mark Comparison Answers)

| Feature | Vapour Pressure | Gas (Const V) | Bimetallic | RTD | Thermocouple |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output signal** | Pressure | Pressure | Mechanical | Resistance | EMF (mV) |
| **Linearity** | Poor | Excellent (K) | Moderate | Excellent | Moderate |
| **Accuracy** | ±0.5 to 2 % | ±0.1 to 0.5 % | ±1 to 2 % | ±0.1 to 0.5 % | ±0.5 to 2 % |
| **Sensitivity** | High near boil pt. | Linear | High | High | High |
| **Range** | Narrow/Wide | Widest | Moderate | Wide | Widest |
| **Response time** | Moderate | Slow | Moderate | Fast (wire) | Fastest |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Very low | Moderate | Low |
| **Self-powered?** | No (needs pressure) | No | No | No (needs excitation) | **YES** |
| **Cold-junction compensation?** | No | No | No | No | **Required** |
| **Remote measurement?** | Yes (capillary) | Yes | No | Yes (wires) | Yes (wires) |
| **Typical fill / material** | Methyl chloride, Butane | N₂, He | Brass-Invar | Pt, Cu, Ni | K, J, T, etc. |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4]**
*Define the Seebeck Effect. Name the two junctions of a thermocouple circuit.*

**Model Answer:**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the phenomenon of generation of an electromotive force (EMF) in a closed circuit formed by two dissimilar conductors or semiconductors when their two junctions are maintained at different temperatures. The magnitude of the EMF depends on the materials used and the temperature difference between the junctions.

The two junctions are:
- **Hot Junction (Measuring Junction):** Exposed to the temperature $T_2$ being measured.
- **Cold Junction (Reference Junction):** Maintained at a known reference temperature $T_1$ (conventionally 0 °C using an ice bath).

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's tip:** Do not confuse with the Peltier Effect. Mention the year 1821 and Seebeck's name for full marks.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]**
*What are the active and passive metals in a bimetallic strip? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

In a bimetallic strip, two metals with **different coefficients of linear expansion** are bonded together. The metal with the **higher** coefficient of expansion is called the **active metal** (it expands more on heating), while the metal with the **lower** coefficient of expansion is called the **passive metal** (it expands less and acts as the constraint).

| Component | Role | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Active Metal | Higher $\alpha$ | **Brass** ($\alpha = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/°C) |
| Passive Metal | Lower $\alpha$ | **Invar** ($\alpha = 1 \times 10^{-6}$/°C) |

The strip bends **towards the passive side** (lower expansion) on heating.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **Vapour Pressure Thermometer**. Derive the relationship between vapour pressure and temperature. (7 marks)

**(b)** A bimetallic strip is made of brass ($\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/°C) and Invar ($\alpha_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/°C). The strip length is 250 mm and total thickness 0.6 mm. Calculate (i) the angular deflection for $\Delta T = 120\ °C$ and (ii) the tip displacement. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) — Vapour Pressure Thermometer

**Construction:**
The vapour pressure thermometer consists of three main parts:
1. **Bulb (Sensing Element)** — A thin-walled metallic (stainless steel) bulb containing a volatile liquid (e.g., methyl chloride, butane) **partially filled**. The space above the liquid is filled with saturated vapour of the same liquid.
2. **Capillary Tube** — A small-bore stainless steel tube transmitting pressure from the bulb to the indicating device. It is fully filled with the vapour (or with an inert gas for compensation).
3. **Bourdon Tube (Indicator)** — A curved metallic tube that straightens under internal pressure, providing mechanical displacement proportional to pressure.

**Working Principle:**
1. The bulb is exposed to the process whose temperature is to be measured.
2. The volatile liquid in the bulb vaporizes, and the saturated vapour above the liquid attains equilibrium at the new temperature.
3. The **saturation vapour pressure** is a **unique function** of temperature (single-valued relationship).
4. This pressure is transmitted through the capillary to the Bourdon tube.
5. The Bourdon tube deflects, and through a mechanical linkage, drives a pointer over a calibrated scale showing temperature.

**Derivation (Clausius–Clapeyron Equation):**
At the liquid-vapour interface, considering equilibrium and applying thermodynamic relations:

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{L}{T(V_g - V_l)}$$

where $L$ = latent heat of vaporization per mole, $V_g$ = molar volume of vapour, $V_l$ = molar volume of liquid.

Assuming $V_g \gg V_l$ and ideal gas behavior ($V_g = RT/P$):

$$\frac{dP}{dT} = \frac{LP}{RT^2}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L}{R}\cdot\frac{dT}{T^2}$$

Integrating between two states (assuming $L$ constant):

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \frac{dP}{P} = \frac{L}{R} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \frac{dT}{T^2}$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{L}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

This shows the **exponential** dependence of vapour pressure on temperature. *(7 marks — sketch + working + derivation)*

#### Part (b) — Bimetallic Strip Numerical

**Given:**
- $\alpha_1 = 19 \times 10^{-6}$/°C (brass)
- $\alpha_2 = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}$/°C (Invar)
- $L = 250\ \text{mm} = 0.25\ \text{m}$
- $t = 0.6\ \text{mm} = 0.6 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$
- $\Delta T = 120\ °C$

**Step 1: Compute differential expansion coefficient.**
$$\Delta \alpha = \alpha_1 - \alpha_2 = (19 - 1.2) \times 10^{-6} = 17.8 \times 10^{-6}\ /^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 2: Compute angular deflection $\theta$.**
$$\theta = \frac{360 \cdot L \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{\pi \cdot t}$$

$$\theta = \frac{360 \times 0.25 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{\pi \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3: Numerator.**
$$360 \times 0.25 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 120 = 0.19224$$

**Step 4: Denominator.**
$$\pi \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.8850 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 5: Divide.**
$$\theta = \frac{0.19224}{1.8850 \times 10^{-3}} = 101.99\ ^\circ \approx 102\ ^\circ$$

**Step 6: Tip displacement $\delta$ (cantilever approximation).**
$$\delta = \frac{L^2 \cdot \Delta \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{2t}$$

$$\delta = \frac{(0.25)^2 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 120}{2 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 7: Numerator.**
$$0.0625 \times 17.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 120 = 1.335 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 8: Denominator.**
$$2 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 9: Divide.**
$$\delta = \frac{1.335 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.11125\ \text{m} = 111.25\ \text{mm}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Angular deflection $\theta \approx 102°$
- (ii) Tip displacement $\delta \approx 111.3\ \text{mm}$ *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to convert $L$ and $t$ to **consistent SI units** (m). Always use meters.
> 2. ❌ Mixing °C and K in the temperature difference (ΔT is the same in both).
> 3. ❌ Using $\theta$ in radians in the wrong formula — the formula given above yields $\theta$ in **degrees**.
> 4. ❌ Forgetting to identify active vs passive metal — strip bends **towards passive (low α) side**.

---

### ❓ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (ALTERNATIVE) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]**

**(a)** Explain the **Seebeck Effect** and derive the expression for thermoelectric EMF. State and explain the **Law of Intermediate Metals** and the **Law of Intermediate Temperatures** with their significance in thermocouple instrumentation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A platinum RTD (Pt-100) has $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C. Using the **Callendar–Van Dusen equation**, find its resistance at (i) 100 °C and (ii) −100 °C. Given: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$. (7 marks)

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) — Seebeck Effect and Thermocouple Laws

**Seebeck Effect:**
The Seebeck Effect, discovered by **Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821**, states that when two dissimilar conductors A and B are joined at their ends to form a closed circuit, and the two junctions are maintained at **different temperatures** $T_1$ (cold) and $T_2$ (hot), an **electromotive force (EMF)** is generated in the circuit, causing a current to flow.

**Derivation:**
The Seebeck EMF in a circuit with two conductors A and B at junctions $T_1$ and $T_2$ is given by integrating the difference in Seebeck coefficients over the temperature range:

$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \left[S_A(T) - S_B(T)\right] dT$$

where $S_A$ and $S_B$ are the **Seebeck coefficients** (thermoelectric powers) of materials A and B in µV/°C. If $S_A$ and $S_B$ are approximately constant over the range:

$$E_{AB} = (S_A - S_B)(T_2 - T_1) = S_{AB} \cdot \Delta T$$

**Law of Intermediate Metals:**
*"The insertion of a third (or any number of) metal(s) into a thermocouple circuit does not change the net EMF, provided both ends of the inserted metal are at the same temperature."*

**Significance:** This law permits the use of **connecting wires, terminal blocks, and measuring instruments** (which introduce extra metals) in the thermocouple circuit without introducing errors. It also enables **soldering, brazing, or welding** of thermocouple junctions without affecting the calibration.

Mathematically:
$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) = E_{AC}(T_1, T_2) + E_{CB}(T_1, T_2)$$

**Law of Intermediate Temperatures:**
*"The EMF generated by a thermocouple with junctions at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_3$ is equal to the algebraic sum of the EMFs generated by the same thermocouple with junctions at $(T_1, T_2)$ and $(T_2, T_3)$."*

**Significance:** This law is the foundation of **cold-junction compensation (CJC)**. It allows reference tables (calibrated at 0 °C) to be used with the actual cold-junction at a different temperature. We simply add the EMF corresponding to (cold-junction temp − 0 °C) to the measured EMF.

Mathematically:
$$E_{AB}(T_1, T_3) = E_{AB}(T_1, T_2) + E_{AB}(T_2, T_3)$$ *(7 marks)*

#### Part (b) — RTD Callendar–Van Dusen Numerical

**Given:** $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7}$, $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12}$.

**Part (i) — At $t = 100\ °C$ (use 0–850 °C form):**
$$R_t = R_0(1 + At + Bt^2)$$

**Step 1: $At$ term.**
$$At = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.39083$$

**Step 2: $Bt^2$ term.**
$$Bt^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times (100)^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-3} = -0.005775$$

**Step 3: Sum.**
$$1 + 0.39083 - 0.005775 = 1.385055$$

**Step 4: Multiply by $R_0$.**
$$R_{100} = 100 \times 1.385055 = 138.51\ \Omega$$

**Part (ii) — At $t = -100\ °C$ (use below-0 form):**
$$R_t = R_0\left[1 + At + Bt^2 + C(t - 100)t^3\right]$$

**Step 5: $At$ term.**
$$At = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \times (-100) = -0.39083$$

**Step 6: $Bt^2$ term.**
$$Bt^2 = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \times 10000 = -0.005775$$

**Step 7: Compute $(t-100)t^3$.**
$$(t-100)t^3 = (-100 - 100) \times (-100)^3 = (-200) \times (-1{,}000{,}000) = 2 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 8: $C(t-100)t^3$ term.**
$$C(t-100)t^3 = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \times 2 \times 10^{8} = -8.366 \times 10^{-4} = -0.0008366$$

**Step 9: Sum all terms inside brackets.**
$$1 + (-0.39083) + (-0.005775) + (-0.0008366) = 0.60256$$

**Step 10: Multiply by $R_0$.**
$$R_{-100} = 100 \times 0.60256 = 60.26\ \Omega$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $R_{100°C} = 138.51\ \Omega$
- (ii) $R_{-100°C} = 60.26\ \Omega$ *(7 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. ❌ Using the wrong form of the Callendar-Van Dusen equation for negative temperatures. The cubic term appears **only for $t < 0\ °C$**.
> 2. ❌ Sign errors in the cubic term — $(t-100) \cdot t^3$ must be computed carefully, especially for negative $t$.
> 3. ❌ Forgetting to mention that Pt-100's nominal resistance is **100 Ω at 0 °C** (the standard IEC 60751 specifies this).
> 4. ❌ Confusing the two thermocouple laws — Intermediate **Metals** deals with *materials*, Intermediate **Temperatures** deals with *junction temperatures*. Examiners often give 0 marks if these are swapped.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before the KTU exam)**

- ✅ **Vapour Pressure Thermometer:** Works on saturation vapour pressure; **non-linear**; use **Clausius–Clapeyron** equation; volatile liquid partially fills the bulb.
- ✅ **Gas Thermometer (Constant V):** Uses **Gay-Lussac's Law** ($P \propto T$ in Kelvin); **linear** in K; inert gas (N₂, He).
- ✅ **Bimetallic Strip:** Two metals with **different $\alpha$** bonded; bends towards **passive (low α) side**; common pair is **Brass–Invar**.
- ✅ **Bimetallic Angular Deflection:** $\theta = \dfrac{360 \cdot L \cdot (\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T}{\pi \cdot t}$ (in degrees).
- ✅ **RTD:** Resistance increases with temperature; **Pt-100** is the standard ($R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at 0 °C, $\alpha = 0.00385$/°C).
- ✅ **Callendar-Van Dusen:** $R_t = R_0(1 + At + Bt^2)$ for $0–850\ °C$; cubic term added for $t < 0$.
- ✅ **Seebeck Effect (1821):** Two dissimilar metals + temperature difference → EMF.
- ✅ **Three Thermoelectric Effects:** Seebeck (1821), Peltier (1834), Thomson (1856).
- ✅ **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Extra metal OK if both ends at same temperature → enables lead wires.
- ✅ **Law of Intermediate Temperatures:** $E(T_1, T_3) = E(T_1, T_2) + E(T_2, T_3)$ → basis of CJC.
- ✅ **Standard Thermocouple Types (ITS-90):** B, E, J, K, R, S, T — **K-type** is most common industrially.
- ✅ **RTD vs Thermocouple:** RTD is more accurate and linear but needs excitation; Thermocouple is self-powered and works at much higher temperatures but needs CJC.
- ✅ **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Add EMF corresponding to actual cold-junction temperature to the measured EMF before using reference tables calibrated at 0 °C.
- ✅ **Units to remember:** Seebeck coefficient in **µV/°C** or **V/K**; α in **/°C**; R in **Ω**; t in **mm** for bimetallic (convert to m for SI calculation).
- ✅ **Common Pitfall:** ΔT is the same in °C and K — do not add 273.15 to the difference.
- ✅ **Gas Thermometer Answer Trick:** When pressure ratio = 1.365, temperature = 100 °C (boiling point of water).

---

*End of Module 4 Notes — PEFTT638 PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Peltier Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT638 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Peltier Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Peltier Effect (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The **Peltier Effect** is the reversible thermoelectric phenomenon in which **heat is either absorbed or liberated at the junction of two dissimilar conductors** when a **direct electric current** is passed through it. The direction of heat flow is governed by the direction of current flow through the junction. It is the **inverse (reciprocal) phenomenon of the Seebeck Effect**.

Mathematically, the rate of Peltier heat ($Q_P$) absorbed or evolved at a junction is given by:

$$Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$$

where $\pi_{ab}$ is the **Peltier coefficient** of conductor *a* with respect to conductor *b* (in Volts), and $I$ is the current flowing through the junction (in Amperes).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Sided Drinking Glass" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition**
> Imagine a tiny junction (the meeting point of two different metal wires) as a **two-sided drinking glass**. When you force an electric current through this junction, one side of the glass "fills up" with thermal energy (gets **HOT**) while the other side "empties out" its thermal energy (gets **COLD**). If you reverse the current direction, the hot and cold sides simply **swap**. This is precisely how a small portable refrigerator cooler or a CPU heatsink in laptops works at the microscopic level!

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit of Peltier Coefficient ($\pi$):** **Volt (V)**
- **Alternative Unit:** **Watt per Ampere (W/A)** (since $Q_P = \pi \cdot I$)
- **Typical Peltier Coefficient values for metal pairs:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ V (very small for metals)
- **Typical values for semiconductor pairs (e.g., Bi$_2$Te$_3$):** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$ V (much larger, making them practically useful)
- **Typical operating currents for Peltier modules:** **1 A to 6 A**
- **Standard operating voltage:** **3.7 V to 15.4 V DC**
- **Figure of Merit (Z):** $Z = \frac{S^2 \cdot \sigma}{\kappa}$ where $S$ is Seebeck coefficient, $\sigma$ is electrical conductivity, $\kappa$ is thermal conductivity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The Peltier coefficient $\pi_{ab}$ is **NOT** a property of the junction alone — it is a **relative property** of the two conductors forming the junction. Just like the Seebeck coefficient, it is a **paired quantity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Why does the Peltier Effect Occur? (The "How")

- **Step 1 — Carrier Energy Levels:** Different conductors possess **different Fermi energy levels** ($E_F$). When two dissimilar metals/semiconductors are joined, the conduction electrons in each material sit at different average energy states.
- **Step 2 — Current Flow Injection:** When an external DC source forces current through the junction, electrons (charge carriers) are **injected** from the material with the higher Fermi level into the material with the lower Fermi level.
- **Step 3 — Energy Exchange at Junction:** Upon crossing the boundary, each electron must either **release energy** (dropping to a lower energy level → produces **heat** → junction warms) or **absorb energy** (being lifted to a higher level → absorbs **heat** → junction cools).
- **Step 4 — Direction Reversibility:** Reversing the current polarity inverts the carrier flow direction, which consequently **swaps the hot and cold junctions** — demonstrating the **reversible nature** of the Peltier effect.
- **Step 5 — Pure Effect Isolation:** The Peltier heat is **distinct** from Joule heating ($I^2R$ losses). Joule heat is always positive and irreversible, while Peltier heat is reversible and changes sign with current direction.

### 2.1.2 Why is it the Inverse of the Seebeck Effect?

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermodynamic Reciprocity (Kelvin Relations)**
> The Seebeck and Peltier effects are linked by the **Onsager Reciprocal Relations** (also called the **Kelvin Relations**):
> $$\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$$
> where $S_{ab}$ is the Seebeck coefficient and $T$ is the absolute temperature (in Kelvin). This means a good Seebeck material is automatically a good Peltier material!

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$ | Rate of Peltier heat absorption/liberation at a junction | W (Watts) |
| 2 | $\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$ | Kelvin relation: Peltier coefficient from Seebeck coefficient | V (Volts) |
| 3 | $\pi_{a} - \pi_{b} = \pi_{ab}$ | Relative Peltier coefficient of materials *a* and *b* | V (Volts) |
| 4 | $Z = \frac{S^2 \sigma}{\kappa}$ | Figure of Merit for thermoelectric materials | $\text{K}^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{net}} = \pi I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R + K \Delta T$ | Net heat pumped by a Peltier cooler (with Joule + Fourier losses) | W |
| 6 | $dQ/dt = \pi_{ab} I - I^2 R/2$ | Heat absorbed at cold junction of a Peltier cooler | W |
| 7 | $T_{\text{hot}} = T_{\text{cold}} + \frac{\pi I - I^2 R/2}{K}$ | Temperature difference across a Peltier module | K |
| 8 | $V_{\text{emf}} = S_{ab} \Delta T$ | Seebeck EMF (reciprocal phenomenon) | V |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Do NOT confuse $Q_P$ (Peltier heat, linear in $I$) with $Q_J = I^2 R$ (Joule heat, quadratic in $I$). They are physically **different** heat generation mechanisms. Joule heat is always positive; Peltier heat changes sign with current.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Peltier Effect is the working principle behind:

1. **Thermoelectric Coolers (TECs)** — Small-scale refrigeration for laser diodes, infrared detectors, and CPU overclocking rigs.
2. **Portable Beverage Coolers & Car Refrigerators** — Camping and road-trip cooling gadgets.
3. **PCR Thermal Cyclers in Biotechnology** — Rapid heating/cooling of DNA samples for COVID-19 testing.
4. **Dehumidifiers & Environmental Chambers** — Precise dew-point control.
5. **Spacecraft Thermal Management** — NASA's deep-space probes (e.g., Curiosity rover) use radioisotope-powered Peltier generators working in reverse (Seebeck mode).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship between Peltier and Seebeck Coefficients (Kelvin Relation)

This derivation is a **high-yield KTU derivation** and frequently appears in board exams.

### Given Premise
We treat the thermocouple as a reversible thermodynamic engine. The Seebeck effect generates EMF ($S_{ab} \Delta T$) when a temperature gradient is applied, and the Peltier effect pumps heat ($\pi_{ab} I$) when a current flows.

### Step 1: Thermodynamic Setup
Consider a single thermocouple loop with junctions held at temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold), with $T_1 > T_2$. The EMF generated by Seebeck effect is:

$$\mathcal{E} = S_{ab} (T_1 - T_2)$$

### Step 2: Work Done in a Reversible Cycle
When the circuit drives a current $I$ through the load, charge $dq = I \, dt$ is transported. The work done by the EMF source is:

$$dW = \mathcal{E} \, dq = S_{ab} (T_1 - T_2) \, I \, dt$$

### Step 3: Peltier Heat Absorbed and Liberated
At the hot junction ($T_1$), heat $Q_1 = \pi_{ab} I$ is released, and at the cold junction ($T_2$), heat $Q_2 = \pi_{ab} I$ is absorbed (depending on sign convention).

### Step 4: Apply First Law of Thermodynamics
For a reversible process, the total entropy change of the universe must be zero:

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \frac{Q_1}{T_1} - \frac{Q_2}{T_2} = 0$$

Substituting the Peltier heat expressions:

$$\frac{\pi_{ab} I}{T_1} - \frac{\pi_{ab} I}{T_2} = 0$$

This appears trivially zero, but we must also account for the **work-entropy coupling** using Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1}$$

The mechanical work produced must equal the Carnot-equivalent heat transport:

$$dW = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \cdot Q_1 = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} \cdot \pi_{ab} I \, dt$$

### Step 5: Equating Both Work Expressions

Setting the two work expressions equal:

$$S_{ab} (T_1 - T_2) I = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1} \pi_{ab} I$$

### Step 6: Cancellation and Final Result

Cancel the common factor $(T_1 - T_2) I$ from both sides:

$$S_{ab} = \frac{\pi_{ab}}{T_1}$$

Since this must hold at any junction temperature $T$, we replace $T_1$ with $T$:

$$\boxed{\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T}$$

This is the celebrated **First Kelvin Relation**, proving that Peltier and Seebeck coefficients are fundamentally linked.

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Balance at the Cold Junction of a Peltier Cooler

### Given
A Peltier module is supplied with current $I$. It exhibits Peltier heat absorption $\pi I$, internal electrical resistance $R$, and thermal conductance $K$ between the hot and cold sides.

### Step 1: Peltier Heat Absorbed at Cold Junction
The rate of heat absorbed due to Peltier effect:

$$Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$$

### Step 2: Joule Heat (I²R) Distribution
By symmetry, half of the Joule heat flows back to the cold junction and half flows to the hot junction:

$$Q_{J,\text{cold}} = \frac{1}{2} I^2 R$$

### Step 3: Fourier Heat Leakage (Back-Conduction)
Heat leaks from the hot side to the cold side via thermal conduction:

$$Q_{\text{cond}} = K (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})$$

### Step 4: Steady-State Energy Balance at Cold Junction
At steady state, the net cooling power at the cold junction is:

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{cool}} = \pi_{ab} I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K (T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}})}$$

### Step 5: Maximum Temperature Difference
Setting $Q_{\text{cool}} = 0$ (no useful cooling extracted, maximum $\Delta T$) and differentiating with respect to $I$ to find the optimum current:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{dI} = \frac{\pi - IR}{K} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_{\text{opt}} = \frac{\pi}{R}$$

Substituting back yields the **maximum achievable temperature difference**:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2}{2 K R} = \frac{1}{2} Z T_c^2}$$

This is why the **Figure of Merit $Z$** determines the efficiency ceiling of a Peltier cooler.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — Peltier Effect Mechanism

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the causal sequence governing the Peltier Effect, from external current injection to heat manifestation at the junction.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Current Source I Applied] --> B{Current Direction Polarity}
    B -- Forward Polarity --> C[Electrons Injected from Material A]
    B -- Reverse Polarity --> D[Electrons Injected from Material B]
    C --> E{Junction Energy Level Comparison}
    D --> E
    E -- Electrons Drop to Lower Energy --> F[Heat Released at Junction]
    E -- Electrons Rise to Higher Energy --> G[Heat Absorbed at Junction]
    F --> H[Junction Becomes HOT]
    G --> I[Junction Becomes COLD]
    H --> J[Peltier Coefficient Positive pi_ab]
    I --> K[Peltier Coefficient Effective Cooling]
    J --> L[Result: Q_P equals pi_ab times I Watts]
    K --> L
    L --> M[Reverse Current to Swap Hot Cold Sides]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Thermoelectric Peltier Module

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M1[Thermoelectric Module TEC1-12706]
        node1[N-Type Semiconductor Pellet] --> node2[Junction Hot Side Ceramic Plate]
        node3[P-Type Semiconductor Pellet] --> node2
        node2 --> node4[Junction Cold Side Ceramic Plate]
        node4 --> node5[External Load to be Cooled]
        node1 -. Copper Strap .-> node3
    end
    node6[External DC Power Supply V DC] --> node1
    node6 --> node3
    node7[Heat Sink with Forced Air Cooling] -. Removes Heat From .-> node2
```

## 4.3 Schematic Representation of a Basic Peltier Junction

```
        Conductor A (Material 1)         Conductor B (Material 2)
        ─────────────────────────      ─────────────────────────
                       \                      /
                        \                    /
                         \       JUNCTION   /
                          \      ← I →     /
                           ●──────────────●
                          HOT or COLD     (Temperature change occurs HERE)
                           ●──────────────●
                          /                \
                         /                  \
        ────────────────/────────────────────\────────────────
              Heat Absorbed OR Liberated = π_ab × I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:** A single junction shows two dissimilar conductors meeting at a point. The direction of current $I$ decides whether this junction absorbs heat (acting as a cooler) or releases heat (acting as a heater). For practical cooling, multiple such junctions are cascaded into a thermoelectric module.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the Peltier Effect. State its SI unit and the governing mathematical relation.**

**Model Answer:**

The Peltier Effect is defined as the **phenomenon of heat absorption or liberation at the junction of two dissimilar conductors when an electric current is passed through it**. It is the **converse of the Seebeck Effect**. The direction of heat flow depends on the direction of current. The governing equation is:

$$Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$$

The **SI unit of Peltier coefficient $\pi_{ab}$ is the Volt (V)**. Alternatively, it can be expressed in **Watts per Ampere (W/A)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the unit explicitly. Marks are awarded separately for the formula AND for stating the unit. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for unit.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between the Peltier Effect and the Seebeck Effect.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Peltier Effect | Seebeck Effect |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Cause | Electric current is applied | Temperature difference is applied |
| Result | Heat is absorbed/liberated at junction | EMF is generated |
| Direction of heat/EMF | Depends on current direction | Depends on hot/cold junction |
| Governing equation | $Q_P = \pi_{ab} I$ | $\mathcal{E} = S_{ab} \Delta T$ |
| Application | Thermoelectric coolers (TECs) | Thermocouples, temperature sensors |
| Reversibility | Reversible | Reversible |

Both phenomena are mathematically linked by the **Kelvin Relation**: $\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the Peltier coefficient and the Seebeck coefficient of a thermocouple using thermodynamic principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermoelectric cooler made of Bismuth Telluride (Bi$_2$Te$_3$) has a Peltier coefficient of $0.1$ V at $T = 300$ K. If a current of 5 A is passed through it, calculate:
  - (i) The rate of heat absorption at the cold junction (neglecting Joule heating). **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) The corresponding Seebeck coefficient. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) The EMF generated if the same module experiences a temperature difference of 40 K. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Refer to **Section 3.1** above. The complete step-by-step derivation yields the **First Kelvin Relation**:

$$\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$$

The derivation involves:
- Equating the work done by the Seebeck EMF with the Carnot-equivalent heat transport.
- Applying the principle of zero net entropy change in a reversible thermodynamic cycle.
- Canceling common terms $(T_1 - T_2) I$ to obtain the final relation.

*(Full step-by-step reasoning as detailed in Section 3.1 must be written on the answer sheet for full marks.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Peltier coefficient: $\pi_{ab} = 0.1$ V
- Absolute temperature: $T = 300$ K
- Current: $I = 5$ A
- Temperature difference: $\Delta T = 40$ K

**(i) Rate of heat absorption at the cold junction:**

Using the Peltier equation $Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$:

$$Q_P = 0.1 \times 5 = 0.5 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** $Q_P = \mathbf{0.5 \text{ W}}$ of heat is absorbed at the cold junction.

**(ii) Corresponding Seebeck coefficient:**

Using the Kelvin relation $\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$:

$$S_{ab} = \frac{\pi_{ab}}{T} = \frac{0.1}{300} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}$$

**Answer:** $S_{ab} = \mathbf{3.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V/K}}$

**(iii) EMF generated for $\Delta T = 40$ K:**

Using the Seebeck equation $\mathcal{E} = S_{ab} \cdot \Delta T$:

$$\mathcal{E} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 40 = 0.0133 \text{ V} = 13.33 \text{ mV}$$

**Answer:** $\mathcal{E} = \mathbf{13.33 \text{ mV}}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a thermoelectric cooler based on the Peltier Effect, with the help of a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Peltier module has the following specifications: Peltier coefficient $\pi = 0.08$ V, internal resistance $R = 2 \, \Omega$, and thermal conductance $K = 0.5$ W/K. If a current of 4 A is supplied:
  - (i) Calculate the rate of Joule heating inside the module. **(2 marks)**
  - (ii) Calculate the net cooling power at the cold junction, assuming $T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}} = 20$ K. **(2 marks)**
  - (iii) Calculate the maximum achievable temperature difference $\Delta T_{\max}$. **(3 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

A thermoelectric cooler (TEC) consists of the following components:
1. **P-type and N-type semiconductor pellets** (typically Bismuth Telluride, Bi$_2$Te$_3$) — chosen because semiconductors have much higher Peltier coefficients than metals.
2. **Junctions formed by metallization** between the P and N pellets using copper bridges.
3. **Two ceramic plates** (alumina, Al$_2$O$_3$) sandwich the pellets — one acts as the cold side and the other as the hot side.
4. **External DC power supply** drives current through the series-connected PN junctions.
5. **Heat sink** with forced air or water cooling removes heat from the hot side.

**Working Principle:** When DC current flows from N to P at the upper junction, electrons move from P to N across that junction. To maintain energy conservation, the junction **absorbs heat** from its surroundings → becomes cold. At the lower junction, electrons move from N to P, **releasing heat** → becomes hot. The cold side can then cool an external object placed on it.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $\pi = 0.08$ V
- $R = 2 \, \Omega$
- $K = 0.5$ W/K
- $I = 4$ A
- $\Delta T = 20$ K

**(i) Rate of Joule heating:**

$$Q_J = I^2 R = (4)^2 \times 2 = 16 \times 2 = 32 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** $Q_J = \mathbf{32 \text{ W}}$

**(ii) Net cooling power at cold junction:**

Using the heat balance equation $Q_{\text{cool}} = \pi I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$:

$$Q_{\text{cool}} = (0.08)(4) - \frac{1}{2}(4)^2(2) - (0.5)(20)$$

$$Q_{\text{cool}} = 0.32 - 16 - 10 = -25.68 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** $Q_{\text{cool}} = \mathbf{-25.68 \text{ W}}$ (negative sign indicates net cooling is required; magnitude is 25.68 W)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In numerical problems, **never forget the factor of 1/2** for Joule heating distribution. Half goes to the cold side, half goes to the hot side. Losing this factor costs **1 full mark**.

**(iii) Maximum temperature difference:**

Using the formula $\Delta T_{\max} = \frac{\pi^2}{2 K R}$:

$$\Delta T_{\max} = \frac{(0.08)^2}{2 \times 0.5 \times 2} = \frac{0.0064}{2} = 0.0032 \text{ K}$$

**Answer:** $\Delta T_{\max} = \mathbf{0.0032 \text{ K}}$ (which is impractically small, illustrating why high-$Z$ semiconductor materials are essential!)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Peltier Effect Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to state the unit of Peltier coefficient** (it is Volts, NOT Watts). Watts is the unit of $Q_P$, the heat itself.
> 2. **Confusing Peltier heat ($\pi I$, linear) with Joule heat ($I^2 R$, quadratic)** — they are physically distinct. KTU examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **Missing the Kelvin Relation** $\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} T$ when a question links Seebeck and Peltier — this is a **favourite 7-mark derivation question**.
> 4. **Sign convention errors:** Reversing current direction reverses the sign of $\pi_{ab}$ — students often forget this.
> 5. **In numerical problems, ignoring the 1/2 factor** in Joule heat distribution at the cold junction.
> 6. **Not drawing a labelled diagram** for construction-based questions (mandatory for full marks in 7-mark sub-parts).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Peltier Effect = Heat at a junction due to current flow** (Seebeck Effect's reciprocal).
- ✅ Core equation: $Q_P = \pi_{ab} \cdot I$ — linear dependence on current.
- ✅ **Kelvin Relation:** $\pi_{ab} = S_{ab} \cdot T$ — links Peltier and Seebeck coefficients.
- ✅ **Unit of $\pi_{ab}$:** Volt (V); **Unit of $Q_P$:** Watt (W).
- ✅ Peltier heat is **reversible**; Joule heat ($I^2 R$) is **irreversible** and always positive.
- ✅ Practical Peltier coolers use **semiconductors** (Bi$_2$Te$_3$, Sb$_2$Te$_3$) because metals have negligible $\pi$.
- ✅ **Figure of Merit $Z$** dictates efficiency: $Z = \frac{S^2 \sigma}{\kappa}$.
- ✅ Heat balance at cold junction: $Q_{\text{cool}} = \pi I - \frac{1}{2} I^2 R - K \Delta T$.
- ✅ **Maximum $\Delta T$:** $\Delta T_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} Z T_c^2$.
- ✅ Applications: TECs, PCR thermal cyclers, spacecraft RTGs (in reverse), portable coolers.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled diagram** for any construction/explanation question worth 7 marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
