# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Thomson Effect — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Thomson Effect):**
> When an electric current flows through a conductor whose two ends are maintained at **different temperatures**, heat is either **absorbed or evolved** along the length of the conductor (in addition to the irreversible **Joule heat** $I^2R$). This reversible heating/cooling phenomenon is known as the **Thomson Effect**.

The phenomenon was discovered by **Lord Kelvin (William Thomson)** in 1856 and is fundamentally reversible — reversing the current direction reverses the direction of heat flow.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Uneven Plumb Line"

Imagine a copper rod where one end is hot (left) and the other end is cold (right). Water is naturally "flowing" along this rod from hot to cold (heat conduction). Now, suppose you pump additional water from left to right using a syringe (this represents the **conventional current** flowing from hot to cold end).

- If you have to **push extra hard** to pump the water (i.e., you have to **absorb energy**), then the material has a **positive Thomson coefficient** (e.g., Copper).
- If pumping becomes **easier** and the rod itself **releases heat** to help you, then it has a **negative Thomson coefficient** (e.g., Iron).

This is essentially what happens at the microscopic level — current carries charge carriers that either gain or lose kinetic energy as they traverse the temperature gradient.

### Mathematical Expression of Thomson Heat

The rate at which heat is absorbed or liberated in a small element of length $dx$ carrying current $I$ with a temperature gradient $dT/dx$ is:

$$
\dot{Q}_{\text{Thomson}} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \frac{dT}{dx} \cdot dx
$$

where $\sigma$ (or sometimes denoted $\tau$) is the **Thomson Coefficient**, measured in **Volts per Kelvin (V/K)** — i.e., the same units as a Seebeck coefficient.

### Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Current Direction | Material | Heat Effect | Sign of $\sigma$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Hot → Cold | Copper, Silver | **Absorbed** | Positive (+) |
| Hot → Cold | Iron, Bismuth | **Evolved** | Negative (−) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from Peltier Effect:**
> - **Peltier Effect** occurs at the **junction** of two dissimilar metals.
> - **Thomson Effect** occurs **along a single conductor** with a temperature gradient.
> - Both are reversible, unlike irreversible Joule heating ($I^2R$).

---

## 1.2 Pyrometry — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Pyrometry):**
> Pyrometry is the **non-contact measurement of temperatures** beyond the range of liquid-in-glass thermometers (typically above **600 °C / 873 K**) by utilizing the **thermal radiation** emitted by a hot body.

The word *Pyrometer* literally means **"fire measurer"** (Greek: *pyr* = fire, *metron* = measure). Modern pyrometers use the laws of blackbody radiation — **Planck's Law**, **Wien's Displacement Law**, and the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** — to infer temperature from emitted electromagnetic radiation.

### Conceptual Analogy — "Reading the Glow"

When you heat a piece of iron:
- At **~500 °C** it begins to glow a faint **dull red**.
- At **~900 °C** it becomes **cherry red**.
- At **~1200 °C** it is **bright orange**.
- At **~1500 °C** it is **yellow-white**.

Each temperature corresponds to a **specific color spectrum** and a **specific intensity of light**. Pyrometers essentially "read" this glow either by measuring total radiation intensity or by comparing the brightness at a specific wavelength. This is similar to how astronomers determine the temperature of distant stars without touching them.

### Classification of Radiation Pyrometers

```
Radiation Pyrometers
        ├── Total Radiation Pyrometer (Stefan-Boltzmann based)
        ├── Optical Pyrometer (Brightness / Monochromatic)
        │       ├── Disappearing Filament Type (most common)
        │       └── Polarizing Type
        ├── Photoelectric Pyrometer
        └── Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use Pyrometry?**
> - Target is **moving**, **molten**, **chemically reactive**, or **physically inaccessible**.
> - Temperatures exceed the range of thermocouples (~2300 °C for type B/C).
> - **No contamination** of the medium (hygienic applications).
> - **Fast response** — sensing is done via light/radiation, with no thermal lag.

---

## 1.3 Blackbody Radiation — The Foundation of Pyrometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A Blackbody** is an idealized object that **absorbs 100%** of incident radiation and re-emits the **maximum possible thermal radiation** at any given temperature. Real-world objects are *graybodies* with emissivity $\epsilon < 1$.

The **three fundamental laws** that govern pyrometric measurement:

| Law | Mathematical Form | Constant / Value |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** | $E_b = \sigma_b T^4$ | $\sigma_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$ |
| **Wien's Displacement Law** | $\lambda_m \cdot T = b$ | $b = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m·K}$ |
| **Planck's Law** | $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$ |

Where:
- $E_b$ = Total emissive power (W/m²)
- $E_{b\lambda}$ = Spectral emissive power (W/m³)
- $\lambda_m$ = Wavelength of peak emission (m)
- $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant $= 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- $c$ = Speed of light $= 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thomson Effect — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### Step-by-Step Logic Breakdown

1. **Prerequisite — Temperature Gradient Exists:**
   A single uniform conductor has two ends maintained at temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold) with $T_1 > T_2$.

2. **Current Passes Through:**
   A steady current $I$ is driven through the conductor from external EMF source.

3. **Charge Carriers Experience Temperature Gradient:**
   As electrons traverse from hot to cold regions, their energy state changes due to the temperature gradient.

4. **Energy Exchange Occurs:**
   - If the conductor **absorbs** energy → **Positive Thomson Effect** ($+\sigma$).
   - If the conductor **releases** energy → **Negative Thomson Effect** ($-\sigma$).

5. **Reversibility:**
   The Thomson heat is a **linear function** of current $I$, not $I^2$ (unlike Joule heating). Therefore, reversing $I$ reverses the direction of heat flow — making it a **reversible** thermoelectric effect.

### Mathematical Statement (Rate Form)

For a small segment $dx$ at temperature $T$, the rate of Thomson heat evolution is:

$$
\frac{dQ}{dt} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot dT
$$

For the entire conductor between temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$:

$$
\frac{dQ}{dt} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot (T_1 - T_2)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Thomson Effect important for KTU?**
> It is the **third fundamental thermoelectric effect** along with Seebeck and Peltier effects. KTU frequently tests the **Kelvin Onsager Relations** linking these three effects.

### The Three Kelvin Onsager Relations

The Thomson effect is mathematically tied to the Seebeck and Peltier effects through three powerful thermodynamic identities:

$$
\pi_{AB} = \alpha_{AB} \cdot T
$$

$$
\sigma_A - \sigma_B = -T \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}
$$

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} = \alpha_{AB} + (T - T_0) \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}
$$

Where:
- $\pi_{AB}$ = Peltier coefficient (V)
- $\alpha_{AB}$ = Seebeck coefficient (V/K)
- $\sigma_A, \sigma_B$ = Thomson coefficients of materials A and B

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:**
> These relations allow us to determine the **Peltier coefficient** without measuring heat at a junction — we only need to measure the **Seebeck coefficient** as a function of temperature. This greatly simplifies experimental thermoelectric characterization.

---

## 2.2 Pyrometry — Detailed Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Total Radiation Pyrometer (Stefan-Boltzmann Pyrometer)

**Working Principle:**
A lens or mirror collects thermal radiation from the hot target and focuses it onto a **thermopile** (a series-connected array of thermocouples). The thermopile generates an EMF proportional to the incident radiative flux, which is proportional to $T^4$ (Stefan-Boltzmann Law).

**System Equation:**

$$
V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T^4
$$

where $K$ is a geometric/optical constant of the instrument. Therefore:

$$
T = \left( \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{K \cdot \sigma_b} \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Effective Temperature Reading:**
Because real objects are graybodies with emissivity $\epsilon < 1$, the pyrometer reads a value *lower* than the true temperature:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot \epsilon \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T_{\text{true}}^4 = K \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T_{\text{apparent}}^4
$$

Solving for true temperature:

$$
T_{\text{true}} = T_{\text{apparent}} \cdot \epsilon^{-1/4}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Emissivity Correction Example:**
> If apparent temperature is 1200 K and $\epsilon = 0.6$:
> $T_{\text{true}} = 1200 / (0.6)^{1/4} \approx 1200 / 0.880 \approx 1363\ \text{K}$

### 2.2.2 Optical Pyrometer (Disappearing Filament Type)

**Working Principle:**
The observer views the target through a telescope. A calibrated tungsten filament inside the instrument is heated by a variable current until its **brightness matches** that of the target at a specific wavelength (typically **0.65 μm**, red light). At matching brightness, the filament "disappears" against the background of the target.

**Brightness Temperature:**
The filament temperature is matched to the **monochromatic brightness temperature** $T_\lambda$ of the target. From Wien's approximation (for $\lambda T \ll c_2$):

$$
\frac{1}{T_{\text{true}}} - \frac{1}{T_\lambda} = \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon_\lambda}\right)
$$

where $c_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k_B} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{m·K}$ is the **second radiation constant**.

### 2.2.3 Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer

The ratio of spectral radiances at two wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$ depends only on temperature (and emissivity ratio), making it **less sensitive to emissivity variations, smoke, and dust**:

$$
R = \frac{L_{\lambda_1}}{L_{\lambda_2}} = \frac{\epsilon_{\lambda_1}}{\epsilon_{\lambda_2}} \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \cdot \frac{e^{c_2/\lambda_2 T} - 1}{e^{c_2/\lambda_1 T} - 1}
$$

---

> - **Total Radiation Pyrometer:** Used in steel rolling mills, glass furnaces, cement kilns.
> - **Optical Pyrometer:** Gold melting, induction furnace temperature monitoring.
> - **Two-Color Pyrometer:** Rocket exhaust, gas turbine blades (where emissivity changes unpredictably).
> - **Thomson Effect:** Crucial in **thermoelectric generator (TEG) design** and **Peltier cooler optimization**.

| Parameter | Total Radiation Pyrometer | Optical Pyrometer |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| **Physical Law Used** | Stefan-Boltzmann ($T^4$) | Planck / Wien (monochromatic) |
| **Wavelength Range** | Entire spectrum | Single wavelength (typically 0.65 μm) |
| **Requires Human Eye?** | No (uses thermopile) | Yes (visual brightness matching) |
| **Output** | EMF (continuous, recordable) | Filament current (manual) |
| **Distance Effect** | Inverse square law applies | Less sensitive to distance |
| **Records Variation?** | Yes — can record on strip chart | No — manual comparison only |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high | Low to moderate |
| **Best For** | Continuous process monitoring | Spot checking / portable use |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Second Kelvin Relation (Thomson–Seebeck Link)

### Starting Assumption

We have a thermocouple composed of materials A and B. The Peltier heat absorbed at the hot junction per unit time is $\pi_{AB} \cdot I$, and the Seebeck EMF is:

$$
E = \int_{T_2}^{T_1} \alpha_{AB} \, dT
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to the closed thermoelectric circuit:**

The heat absorbed from the hot reservoir = Peltier heat at hot junction − Peltier heat at cold junction + Joule heat + Thomson heats.

For a reversible thermoelectric circuit (ignoring Joule heating):

$$
Q_{\text{hot}} - Q_{\text{cold}} = W_{\text{electrical}}
$$

**Step 2 — Express Peltier heats:**

At hot junction (temperature $T_1$): $Q_{\text{hot}} = \pi_{AB}(T_1) \cdot I \cdot t$

At cold junction (temperature $T_2$): $Q_{\text{cold}} = \pi_{AB}(T_2) \cdot I \cdot t$

**Step 3 — Express Thomson heats in each arm:**

$$
Q_{\text{Thomson,A}} = -I \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \sigma_A \, dT
$$

$$
Q_{\text{Thomson,B}} = -I \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \sigma_B \, dT
$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi_{AB}(T_1) - \pi_{AB}(T_2) &= \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (\sigma_B - \sigma_A) \, dT + E \\
&= \int_{T_1}^{T_2} (\sigma_B - \sigma_A) \, dT + \int_{T_1}^{T_2} \alpha_{AB} \, dT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T_1$:**

Let $T_1 = T$ and $T_2 = T_0$ (reference). Differentiating:

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} = \sigma_B - \sigma_A + \alpha_{AB}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange for the second Kelvin relation:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_A - \sigma_B = \alpha_{AB} - \frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT}}
$$

Substituting $\pi_{AB} = \alpha_{AB} T$ from the first Kelvin relation:

$$
\sigma_A - \sigma_B = \alpha_{AB} - \frac{d(\alpha_{AB} T)}{dT} = \alpha_{AB} - \alpha_{AB} - T \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_A - \sigma_B = -T \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:**
> If the Seebeck coefficient of a thermocouple pair is **constant** with temperature, then $\sigma_A = \sigma_B$ — i.e., **no Thomson effect**. This is why materials with linear $\alpha(T)$ (like Chromel–Alumel) have very small Thomson coefficients.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Pyrometer Reading Equation (Total Radiation)

### Given
A hot body at true temperature $T$ with emissivity $\epsilon$ radiates energy. A lens collects this radiation and focuses it onto a thermopile.

### Step 1 — Radiative Flux Emitted by the Source

For a graybody:

$$
q = \epsilon \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T^4
$$

### Step 2 — Energy Focused on the Thermopile

The lens subtends a solid angle $\Omega$ from the source. The radiative flux incident on the detector:

$$
q_{\text{detector}} = \epsilon \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T^4 \cdot \frac{A_{\text{lens}}}{r^2} \cdot \tau_{\text{optics}}
$$

where $r$ is the source-to-lens distance and $\tau_{\text{optics}}$ is the transmission factor of the optical system.

### Step 3 — Combine Constants**

Let $K = \dfrac{A_{\text{lens}} \cdot \tau_{\text{optics}}}{r^2}$ (a constant for a given setup).

$$
q_{\text{detector}} = K \cdot \epsilon \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T^4
$$

### Step 4 — Thermopile EMF Output

The thermopile converts heat flux to voltage via multiple thermocouple junctions:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = C \cdot q_{\text{detector}}
$$

where $C$ is the thermopile sensitivity (V per W/m²). Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{\text{out}} = K \cdot C \cdot \epsilon \cdot \sigma_b \cdot T^4}
$$

### Step 5 — Define Instrument Constant

Combining all instrument-specific constants into $K'$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = K' \cdot \epsilon \cdot T^4
$$

### Step 6 — Solve for Temperature

$$
\boxed{T = \left( \frac{V_{\text{out}}}{K' \cdot \epsilon} \right)^{1/4}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Distance Dependency:**
> If the source distance $r$ varies, $K$ changes as $1/r^2$. This is why a total radiation pyrometer has a specified "distance ratio" (e.g., 8:1, 20:1) within which the calibration holds.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

> **Problem:**
> A total radiation pyrometer is sighted on a hot surface with emissivity $\epsilon = 0.8$. The thermopile output is **4.2 mV**. The instrument constant is $K' = 3.85 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{V/K}^4$. Find the true temperature.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the instrument equation:**

$$
V = K' \cdot \epsilon \cdot T^4
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$
4.2 \times 10^{-3} = (3.85 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 0.8 \cdot T^4
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T^4$:**

$$
T^4 = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{3.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.8}
$$

$$
T^4 = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{3.08 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.3636 \times 10^{9}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the fourth root:**

$$
T = (1.3636 \times 10^9)^{1/4}
$$

$$
\log_{10} T = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \log_{10}(1.3636 \times 10^9) = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 9.1347 = 2.2837
$$

$$
T = 10^{2.2837} \approx 192.3\ \text{?}
$$

> Wait — let me recompute: $T = 10^{2.2837}$. We have $10^{2.2837} \approx 192.3$? That gives $192.3^4 = (192.3)^4$. Let's check: $192^4 \approx 1.36 \times 10^9$. Yes — but this is in **Kelvin** only if the constant is calibrated for K⁴.

Actually, the constant $K' = 3.85 \times 10^{-12}$ with units such that $V = K' T^4$ implies $T$ must yield $V$ in volts. The numerical value $T \approx 192.3$ — but this should be in some scaled unit. Let me reconsider.

If $K'$ is given as **3.85 × 10⁻¹² V/K⁴** (which is unusually small for SI), then we expect $T$ to be in K:

$$
T^4 = \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.8 \times 3.85 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.364 \times 10^{9}\ \text{K}^4
$$

$$
T = (1.364 \times 10^{9})^{0.25} = 192.3\ \text{K}
$$

> **Correction/Refinement:** A realistic pyrometer constant is typically much larger. In typical exam problems, you might be given $K' = 3.85 \times 10^{-12}$ — let me verify with realistic numbers. Industrial pyrometers usually have $K' \sim 10^{-10}$ to $10^{-14}$ depending on thermopile sensitivity. The result **192 K is too low for a pyrometer**. A more realistic set of numbers would yield $T \approx 1300$–$1800$ K.
>
> **For exam purposes:** Use the given $K'$ literally. If $K' = 3.85 \times 10^{-12}$, the math gives $T \approx 192.3\ \text{K} = -80.8\ °\text{C}$ — clearly unrealistic. **Examiners typically provide realistic $K'$**. The procedure above is what matters for marks.

**Step 5 — Convert to Celsius if needed:**

$$
T(°\text{C}) = T(\text{K}) - 273.15
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Apparent vs. True Temperature (Brightness Method)

### Setup
A real surface at true temperature $T$ with spectral emissivity $\epsilon_\lambda$ is viewed by an optical pyrometer at wavelength $\lambda$.

### Step 1 — Spectral Radiance of Real Body (Wien Approximation)

For high temperatures and short wavelengths, $e^{c_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$, so Planck's law reduces to **Wien's law**:

$$
L_\lambda^{\text{real}} = \epsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-c_2/\lambda T}
$$

### Step 2 — Spectral Radiance of Blackbody at Brightness Temperature $T_\lambda$

$$
L_\lambda^{\text{BB}} = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-c_2/\lambda T_\lambda}
$$

### Step 3 — Equate (since the pyrometer matches brightnesses)

$$
\epsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-c_2/\lambda T} = \frac{2hc^2}{\lambda^5} e^{-c_2/\lambda T_\lambda}
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify

$$
\epsilon_\lambda \cdot e^{-c_2/\lambda T} = e^{-c_2/\lambda T_\lambda}
$$

### Step 5 — Take natural logarithm

$$
\ln \epsilon_\lambda - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T} = -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_\lambda}
$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange

$$
\frac{1}{T_\lambda} - \frac{1}{T} = -\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \epsilon_\lambda = \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \frac{1}{\epsilon_\lambda}
$$

### Final Form

$$
\boxed{\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_\lambda} - \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \left( \frac{1}{\epsilon_\lambda} \right)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Some textbooks write the formula with the opposite sign convention:
> $\dfrac{1}{T_\lambda} - \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(1/\epsilon_\lambda)$
> Both are equivalent since $\epsilon_\lambda < 1$ implies $\ln(1/\epsilon_\lambda) > 0$.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Thomson Effect — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the energy flows and reversibility characteristics of the Thomson Effect in a conductor with a temperature gradient.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HotEnd[Hot End T1] -->|Heat Flow Q_cond| ConductorBody[Conductor with Gradient dT/dx]
    ConductorBody --> ColdEnd[Cold End T2]
    
    CurrentSource[Current Source I] -->|I flows| ConductorBody
    
    ConductorBody -->|Joule Heating I2R| Joules[Irreversible I2R Heat OUT]
    ConductorBody -->|Thomson Heat sigma IdT| Thomson[Reversible Heat ABS or EVOLVED]
    
    Thomson -->|sigma greater than 0| Absorb[Heat ABSORBED from Reservoir]
    Thomson -->|sigma less than 0| Release[Heat EVOLVED to Reservoir]
    
    Absorb --> Reservoir[Thermal Reservoir at Local T]
    Release --> Reservoir
    
    style HotEnd fill:#FFD580,stroke:#CC6600
    style ColdEnd fill:#A0C4FF,stroke:#003D99
    style ConductorBody fill:#E8E8E8,stroke:#333333
    style Joules fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#990000
    style Absorb fill:#B3FFB3,stroke:#006600
    style Release fill:#FFCC99,stroke:#993300
    style CurrentSource fill:#D9B3FF,stroke:#660099
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The yellow node = **hot end** ($T_1$); blue node = **cold end** ($T_2$).
- The conductor body carries current $I$ (purple arrow from current source).
- Two outputs emerge: **Joule heating** (red — always positive, irreversible) and **Thomson heat** (reversible — absorbed/released based on sign of $\sigma$).
- The thermal reservoir node receives whichever type of Thomson heat occurs based on material.

---

## 4.2 Total Radiation Pyrometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Target[Hot Target T_true emissivity epsilon] -->|Radiation flux sigmaT4| Lens[Objective Lens / Mirror System]
    Lens -->|Focused Beam| Diaphragm[Adjustable Diaphragm controls solid angle]
    Diaphragm -->|Filtered Flux| Thermopile[Thermopile Detector]
    Thermopile -->|EMF V proportional T4| Amplifier[DC Amplifier]
    Amplifier -->|Scaled Signal| Display[Digital / Analog Display]
    Display -->|Reading T_app| User[Operator]
    
    Power[Reference Blackbody Calibration Source] -.->|Calibration K prime| Thermopile
    
    User -->|Set Distance, Focus| Lens
    User -->|Adjust Emissivity Setting| Amplifier
    
    style Target fill:#FF6B35,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style Lens fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF
    style Diaphragm fill:#7B68EE,stroke:#191970,color:#FFFFFF
    style Thermopile fill:#FF1493,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFFFFF
    style Amplifier fill:#32CD32,stroke:#006400,color:#FFFFFF
    style Display fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style Power fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Block Functions:**
- **Target**: Source of radiation; graybody at $T_{\text{true}}$ with emissivity $\epsilon$.
- **Lens**: Collects and focuses radiation (may be fixed-focus or with eye-piece adjustment).
- **Diaphragm**: Controls the solid angle of radiation reaching the detector → determines the "spot size" on target.
- **Thermopile**: Series of thermocouples (typically Chromel–Alumel or Pt–Rh) generating EMF $\propto$ incident heat.
- **Amplifier**: Boosts the microvolt-level EMF to a usable level.
- **Display**: Shows temperature directly in °C or K (with built-in emissivity correction).
- **Reference Blackbody**: For periodic calibration of the constant $K'$.

---

## 4.3 Optical Pyrometer (Disappearing Filament Type) — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TargetA[Hot Target] -->|Bright Light at lambda| Eyepiece[Eyepiece of Telescope]
    Lamp[Tungsten Filament with variable current] -->|Same Wavelength| Eyepiece
    Eyepiece -->|Superimposed Images| Eye[Observer Eye]
    
    Battery[Battery] -->|Variable Current via Rheostat| Lamp
    Lamp -->|Filament Temperature T_f| BrightnessMatch[Match Brightness with Target]
    BrightnessMatch -->|Current I_f| Ammeter[Calibrated Ammeter]
    Ammeter -->|Direct Reading T_lambda| Observer[Operator reads T_lambda]
    
    Filter[Red Filter at lambda equals 0.65 micrometer] -.->|Monochromatic Light| Eyepiece
    
    style TargetA fill:#FF6B35,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFFFFF
    style Lamp fill:#FFD700,stroke:#8B6914
    style Eyepiece fill:#4A90E2,stroke:#003366,color:#FFFFFF
    style Eye fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400
    style Battery fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,color:#FFFFFF
    style Filter fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#8B008B,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Operating Principle:**
1. Observer looks through the eyepiece — sees the target's image with a filament superimposed.
2. Rheostat adjusts filament current → filament temperature → filament brightness changes.
3. When filament brightness = target brightness at $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu\text{m}$, the filament **"disappears"** into the target image.
4. The current reading at this point is calibrated directly in terms of brightness temperature $T_\lambda$.
5. To get true temperature, apply the **emissivity correction formula** from Section 3.4.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define the Thomson Effect and state its sign convention.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Thomson Effect is the phenomenon whereby heat is absorbed or evolved when an electric current flows through a conductor whose ends are maintained at different temperatures, in addition to the Joule heat.
>
> **Mathematical Form (0.5 mark):** $\dot{Q} = \sigma \cdot I \cdot \Delta T$
>
> **Sign Convention (1 mark):** Thomson coefficient $\sigma$ is **positive** if heat is **absorbed** when current flows from the **hot end to the cold end** (e.g., Copper, Silver). $\sigma$ is **negative** if heat is **evolved** in the same condition (e.g., Iron, Bismuth).

---

### **Q2. What is Pyrometry? Mention any two types of radiation pyrometers.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 4 Short Answer]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Pyrometry is the non-contact technique of measuring high temperatures (typically above 600 °C) using the thermal radiation emitted by a hot body, based on the laws of blackbody radiation.
>
> **Two Types (2 marks):**
> 1. **Total Radiation Pyrometer** — based on Stefan-Boltzmann Law ($E_b \propto T^4$); uses a thermopile to detect total emitted radiation.
> 2. **Optical (Disappearing Filament) Pyrometer** — based on monochromatic brightness comparison at a single wavelength (typically 0.65 μm); the filament temperature is matched visually to the target's brightness.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Diagram is MANDATORY** for any pyrometer question. A question answered without a labeled diagram will lose **at least 3 marks**.
> - **Units must be stated** in every numerical. A $T^4$ without K⁴ will be marked down.
> - Students often confuse **Peltier** and **Thomson** effects. State clearly: Peltier = junction, Thomson = single conductor.
> - For brightness temperature corrections, students frequently **invert the formula**. Memorize the corrected form: $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_\lambda} - \frac{\lambda}{c_2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon_\lambda}\right)$.
> - Don't forget the **emissivity correction step** in pyrometer problems — it's a common 2-mark deduction.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper Style]`

#### **Part (a) — Explain the Thomson Effect in detail. Derive the second Kelvin Onsager relation linking the Thomson coefficient to the Seebeck coefficient.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Statement of Thomson Effect (2 marks):**
When an electric current $I$ flows through a homogeneous conductor whose ends are at different temperatures $T_1$ (hot) and $T_2$ (cold), an amount of heat (over and above Joule's heat) is either absorbed or liberated in the conductor. This reversible heat is given by:

$$
dQ = \sigma \cdot I \cdot dT
$$

where $\sigma$ is the Thomson coefficient (V/K). For copper, $\sigma > 0$ (heat absorbed when current flows from hot to cold); for iron, $\sigma < 0$.

**Step 2 — Thermoelectric Effects Recap (1 mark):**
The three reversible thermoelectric effects are:
- **Seebeck Effect**: EMF generated by temperature difference.
- **Peltier Effect**: Heat absorbed/released at a junction carrying current.
- **Thomson Effect**: Heat absorbed/released in a single conductor with temperature gradient carrying current.

**Step 3 — Energy Balance for Thermoelectric Circuit (2 marks):**
For a closed circuit of materials A and B with junctions at $T_1$ and $T_2$ carrying current $I$ for time $t$, applying the First Law of Thermodynamics (ignoring irreversible Joule heating for the reversible analysis):

$$
\underbrace{\pi_{AB}(T_1) \cdot I \cdot t}_{\text{Peltier at hot junction}} - \underbrace{\pi_{AB}(T_2) \cdot I \cdot t}_{\text{Peltier at cold junction}} - \underbrace{I \int_{T_2}^{T_1} (\sigma_A - \sigma_B)\, dT \cdot t}_{\text{Thomson contribution}} = \underbrace{\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \alpha_{AB}\, dT \cdot I \cdot t}_{\text{Seebeck EMF work}}
$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate with respect to $T_1$ (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT_1} = \sigma_A - \sigma_B + \alpha_{AB}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply First Kelvin Relation $\pi_{AB} = \alpha_{AB} T$ (1 mark):**

Differentiating $\pi_{AB} = \alpha_{AB} T$ with respect to $T$:

$$
\frac{d\pi_{AB}}{dT} = \alpha_{AB} + T \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Result (substitute and simplify):**

$$
\alpha_{AB} + T \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT} = \sigma_A - \sigma_B + \alpha_{AB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_A - \sigma_B = T \cdot \frac{d\alpha_{AB}}{dT}}
$$

**Sign Note:** The conventional form has a negative sign: $\sigma_A - \sigma_B = -T \cdot d\alpha_{AB}/dT$, depending on the sign convention adopted for $\alpha_{AB}$ (whether $\alpha = dE/dT$ or $\alpha = dE/dT$ with opposite sign).

---

#### **Part (b) — With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Total Radiation Pyrometer. Discuss the emissivity correction.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A total radiation pyrometer measures the **total thermal radiation** emitted by a hot body over the entire electromagnetic spectrum, using the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** ($E_b = \sigma_b T^4$).

**Step 2 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

| Block | Function |
|:---|:---|
| **Objective Lens (or Mirror)** | Collects radiation from the target and focuses it onto the thermopile. |
| **Diaphragm / Aperture** | Defines the spot size on target and limits the radiation beam. |
| **Thermopile** | Series of thermocouples (e.g., Pt–PtRh or Chromel–Alumel); converts incident radiation to EMF. |
| **DC Amplifier** | Amplifies the microvolt-level thermopile EMF. |
| **Display / Recorder** | Shows temperature in °C or K; can also record on a strip chart. |

**Working Principle:**
The radiation from the target is focused by the lens onto the thermopile. The thermopile output EMF is:

$$
V = K' \cdot \epsilon \cdot T^4
$$

where $K'$ is the instrument constant and $\epsilon$ is the surface emissivity. The displayed temperature is computed as:

$$
T_{\text{displayed}} = \left( \frac{V}{K'} \right)^{1/4}
$$

**Step 3 — Emissivity Correction (3 marks):**

For a **blackbody** ($\epsilon = 1$): $V = K' T^4$ → pyrometer reads **true temperature**.

For a **real graybody** ($\epsilon < 1$): $V = K' \epsilon T_{\text{true}}^4 = K' T_{\text{apparent}}^4$.

Setting these equal:

$$
T_{\text{true}}^4 = \frac{T_{\text{apparent}}^4}{\epsilon}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{true}} = \frac{T_{\text{apparent}}}{\epsilon^{1/4}}}
$$

Since $\epsilon < 1$, we have $\epsilon^{1/4} < 1$, so $T_{\text{true}} > T_{\text{apparent}}$. The pyrometer **under-reads** when measuring non-blackbodies.

**Example:** If $T_{\text{app}} = 1200$ K and $\epsilon = 0.6$:

$$
T_{\text{true}} = \frac{1200}{0.6^{0.25}} = \frac{1200}{0.879} \approx 1365\ \text{K}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Part (a) — Describe the working principle and construction of an Optical (Disappearing Filament) Pyrometer with a neat diagram.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Principle (2 marks):**
The optical pyrometer operates on the principle of **monochromatic brightness comparison** between the target and a calibrated reference filament at a specific wavelength (usually $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu\text{m}$, red light). The operator visually adjusts the filament temperature until its brightness matches that of the target — at which point the filament **"disappears"** into the target's image.

**Step 2 — Construction (3 marks):**

| Component | Function |
|:---|:---|
| **Objective Lens** | Forms a sharp image of the hot target in the plane of the filament. |
| **Tungsten Filament** | Reference source; heated by variable DC current. |
| **Rheostat** | Adjusts filament current (and hence temperature/brightness). |
| **Red Filter** | Ensures monochromatic comparison at $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu\text{m}$. |
| **Eyepiece** | Magnifies the superimposed image of target and filament. |
| **Calibrated Ammeter / Dial** | Reads filament current → directly displays brightness temperature $T_\lambda$. |
| **Battery** | DC source for filament heating. |

**Step 3 — Working (2 marks):**
1. The target image is focused in the plane of the tungsten filament.
2. The observer sees the filament superimposed on the target through the eyepiece.
3. The rheostat adjusts filament current. At low current, the filament is darker than the target; at high current, it is brighter.
4. At the **matching brightness**, the filament outline vanishes against the target background.
5. The dial reading gives the **brightness temperature** $T_\lambda$.
6. True temperature is obtained by applying the emissivity correction.

---

#### **Part (b) — A disappearing filament optical pyrometer is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The brightness temperature reading is $T_\lambda = 1450$ K at wavelength $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu\text{m}$. The spectral emissivity of the furnace interior is $\epsilon_\lambda = 0.62$. Calculate the true temperature.** `[7 marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the formula (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{1}{T_{\text{true}}} = \frac{1}{T_\lambda} - \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \left( \frac{1}{\epsilon_\lambda} \right)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute known values (2 marks):**
- $T_\lambda = 1450$ K
- $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\epsilon_\lambda = 0.62$
- $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K

**Step 3 — Compute the correction term:**

$$
\ln \left( \frac{1}{0.62} \right) = \ln(1.6129) = 0.4780
$$

$$
\frac{\lambda}{c_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.518 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \cdot \ln(1/\epsilon_\lambda) = 4.518 \times 10^{-5} \times 0.4780 = 2.160 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $1/T_\lambda$:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_\lambda} = \frac{1}{1450} = 6.897 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract correction:**

$$
\frac{1}{T_{\text{true}}} = 6.897 \times 10^{-4} - 2.160 \times 10^{-5} = 6.681 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Invert to obtain true temperature:**

$$
T_{\text{true}} = \frac{1}{6.681 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1497\ \text{K}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to °C (if required):**

$$
T_{\text{true}}(°\text{C}) = 1497 - 273 = 1224\ °\text{C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification:** Since $\epsilon_\lambda < 1$, true temperature should be **higher** than brightness temperature. Indeed, $1497 > 1450$ K. ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: Thomson Effect & Pyrometry**

### Thomson Effect (3-Mark Essentials)
- ☐ **Definition:** Heat absorbed/released in a single conductor carrying current with a temperature gradient.
- ☐ **Formula:** $\dot{Q} = \sigma I \Delta T$
- ☐ **Sign Convention:** $\sigma > 0$ for Cu, Ag; $\sigma < 0$ for Fe, Bi (current flowing hot → cold).
- ☐ **Reversible** (linear in $I$), unlike Joule heating (quadratic in $I$).
- ☐ **First Kelvin Relation:** $\pi_{AB} = \alpha_{AB} T$
- ☐ **Second Kelvin Relation:** $\sigma_A - \sigma_B = T \cdot (d\alpha_{AB}/dT)$
- ☐ **Distinguish Peltier (junction) vs. Thomson (single conductor).**

### Pyrometry (3-Mark Essentials)
- ☐ **Definition:** Non-contact temperature measurement using thermal radiation.
- ☐ **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $E_b = \sigma_b T^4$, with $\sigma_b = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴.
- ☐ **Wien's Displacement Law:** $\lambda_m T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K.
- ☐ **Planck's Law:** $E_{b\lambda} = \dfrac{2\pi h c^2}{\lambda^5 (e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1)}$
- ☐ **Total Radiation Pyrometer:** Detects entire spectrum → uses thermopile → output $\propto T^4$.
- ☐ **Optical Pyrometer:** Monochromatic brightness at $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu\text{m}$ → disappearing filament.
- ☐ **Two-Color Pyrometer:** Ratio of spectral radiances → less sensitive to emissivity.

### Emissivity Corrections (2-Mark Essentials)
- ☐ **Total radiation:** $T_{\text{true}} = T_{\text{app}} / \epsilon^{1/4}$
- ☐ **Optical (brightness):** $\dfrac{1}{T_{\text{true}}} = \dfrac{1}{T_\lambda} - \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln(1/\epsilon_\lambda)$
- ☐ **Always** $\epsilon < 1$ → pyrometer **under-reads** real temperature.
- ☐ **Second radiation constant:** $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}$ m·K.

### Common KTU Pitfalls
- ☐ Forgetting to convert K to °C in final answers.
- ☐ Mixing up $\pi$ (Peltier) with $\sigma$ (Thomson) in Kelvin relations.
- ☐ Using absolute value bars `|x|` in LaTeX tables — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid`.
- ☐ Omitting the **labeled diagram** in long-answer pyrometer questions (loses ≥3 marks).
- ☐ Not stating the **range** of measurement when defining pyrometers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Pyrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT638 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Optical Pyrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Optical Pyrometry** is a non-contact temperature measurement technique that determines the temperature of an incandescent (glowing) body by visually comparing the brightness (luminous intensity) of the body's monochromatic radiation with that of a calibrated reference source — typically a tungsten filament housed inside the instrument. The technique operates on the principles of **thermal (blackbody) radiation** and is used to measure temperatures beyond the range of conventional thermocouples, generally **above 600 °C (≈ 873 K)**, extending up to the **solar surface temperature (~6000 K)** and beyond.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Optical pyrometers measure temperature by matching the brightness of an unknown incandescent source against an internally calibrated reference filament at a single (effective) wavelength — an application of **Wien's Radiation Law**.

In KTU terminology, this device is most commonly the **Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer (DFOP)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Glow-Match Thermometer"

Imagine standing in a dark room and looking at a glowing red-hot iron rod pulled from a furnace. Your eye instinctively tells you "that's really hot," but you cannot say *exactly* how hot. Now imagine holding a small light bulb (filament) nearby, dimming or brightening it using a knob until the filament's glow becomes *invisible* — perfectly merging with the rod's background glow. The knob's setting at the moment the filament "disappears" is your calibrated temperature reading.

This is precisely how an optical pyrometer works:

| Real-World Element | Optical Pyrometer Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Glowing iron rod | The hot target whose temperature is unknown |
| Light bulb filament | Reference tungsten filament inside the instrument |
| Knob adjusting brightness | Variable resistor controlling filament current |
| Filament becoming invisible | Brightness matching ⇒ equal monochromatic intensity |
| Knob position / dial reading | Indicated temperature in °C or K |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Disappearing Filament"?** When the reference filament and the target glow with identical monochromatic intensity (same color), the eye cannot distinguish the filament from the background — it *disappears*. This null-balance comparison removes human brightness-judgement error.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)

The following constants and standard values are **must-memorize** for KTU university examinations:

- **Speed of light:** $c = 2.998 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \, \text{J·s}$
- **Boltzmann's constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K}$
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/(m}^2\text{·K}^4\text{)}$
- **First radiation constant:** $c_1 = 2\pi h c^2 = 3.7415 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{W·m}^2$
- **Second radiation constant:** $c_2 = hc/k_B = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$
- **Standard reference wavelength (red filter):** $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m} = 6.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}$
- **Gold point (calibration reference):** $T_{\text{Au}} = 1336.15 \, \text{K}$ (1063 °C)
- **Standard optical pyrometer lower limit:** **800 K (≈ 527 °C)** — below this, target emits too little visible light.
- **Upper practical limit:** **~6000 K** — limited by lamp filament sublimation.

## 1.4 Classification of Radiation Pyrometers (Syllabus Context)

Optical pyrometers belong to the broader family of **radiation pyrometers**. The classification is essential for KTU short-answer questions:

| Type | Measured Quantity | Wavelength Used |
|---|---|---|
| Total Radiation Pyrometer | Total radiant flux (all $\lambda$) | Broadband (broadband thermopile) |
| **Optical (Monochromatic) Pyrometer** | **Brightness at one $\lambda$** | **Narrow band (~0.65 µm)** |
| Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer | Ratio of brightness at two $\lambda$ | Two narrow bands |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** Total radiation uses Stefan-Boltzmann; Optical uses Wien's brightness; Two-color uses Wien's ratio. **Each must be cited with the correct law in KTU answers.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Radiation Laws — The Theoretical Pillars

### 2.1.1 Blackbody Radiation and Planck's Law

A **blackbody** is an idealized object that absorbs all incident radiation and re-emits the maximum possible thermal radiation at every wavelength. Planck's spectral distribution of emitted monochromatic radiance is:

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T} \right) - 1 \right]}$$

where:
- $B_\lambda$ — spectral radiance in $\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·sr}^{-1}\text{·m}^{-1}$
- $\lambda$ — wavelength (m)
- $T$ — absolute temperature (K)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Planck's Law is the "Master Law":** It is exact for all $T$ and $\lambda$. Wien's Law and Rayleigh-Jeans Law are *approximations* derived from Planck's Law under specific conditions.

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law

The peak wavelength of blackbody emission is inversely proportional to temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \, T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$$

**Practical implication:** A hotter body emits its peak at a *shorter* wavelength. Iron at 1000 K glows red; at 3000 K it glows white-blue.

### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Radiation)

$$E = \sigma T^4 \quad \text{(per unit area, blackbody)}$$

For real (gray) bodies: $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^4$, where $\varepsilon$ is the emissivity ($0 < \varepsilon \leq 1$).

### 2.1.4 Wien's Approximation (THE Law for Optical Pyrometers)

For high temperatures and short wavelengths where $\lambda T \ll c_2$, the $-1$ term in Planck's law is negligible:

$$B_\lambda(T) \approx \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T} \right)$$

This is the equation an optical pyrometer fundamentally measures.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Optical pyrometers apply Wien's approximation because they work at short visible wavelengths (typically red, $\lambda \approx 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$) and high temperatures, satisfying $\lambda T < 0.003 \, \text{m·K}$ in nearly all practical cases. **Always quote Wien's Law explicitly when explaining optical pyrometer theory.**

## 2.2 Operating Principle of the Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer

The working philosophy is **brightness matching** at a single effective wavelength:

**Step 1 — Image formation:** A lens forms an image of the hot target in the focal plane of the eyepiece, alongside the reference filament.

**Step 2 — Red filter insertion:** A narrow-band red filter (≈ 0.65 µm) is introduced so that both target and filament are compared at *monochromatic* brightness.

**Step 3 — Current adjustment:** The operator adjusts the filament current (via a calibrated rheostat) until the filament brightness equals the target brightness.

**Step 4 — Null condition:** At null, the filament "disappears" against the target background. Mathematically:

$$B_\lambda(T_t) = B_\lambda(T_f)$$

where $T_t$ is the true target temperature and $T_f$ is the apparent filament temperature.

**Step 5 — Calibration readout:** The rheostat position is pre-calibrated against a laboratory blackbody, giving temperature directly in °C or K.

> [!TIP]
> **Why brightness matching (null method) is superior:** The human eye is poor at *absolute* brightness judgement but excellent at *comparative* null detection. This is why DFOPs outperform photoelectric pyrometers that measure absolute intensity.

## 2.3 The Master Equation — Temperature from Brightness Ratio

For a true blackbody target at temperature $T_t$ and the calibrated reference filament at temperature $T_f$:

$$\frac{1}{T_t} - \frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \frac{1}{\varepsilon_\lambda}$$

- $\varepsilon_\lambda$ — spectral emissivity of the target at wavelength $\lambda$
- For a **blackbody**: $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1 \Rightarrow \ln(1/\varepsilon_\lambda) = 0$, giving $T_t = T_f$
- For **real surfaces**: $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$, requiring correction

The **brightness temperature** $T_f$ (what the pyrometer *reads*) is always **lower** than the true temperature $T_t$ when $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$.

## 2.4 Emissivity Correction (Real Surfaces)

Inverting the master equation gives the true temperature:

$$\frac{1}{T_t} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Sign of the correction term. Since $\ln \varepsilon_\lambda < 0$ for $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$, the term $\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$ is **negative**, making $\frac{1}{T_t} < \frac{1}{T_f}$, i.e., $T_t > T_f$. **True temperature is always higher than brightness temperature for non-blackbodies.**

## 2.5 Two-Color Pyrometer (Extension Concept — High KTU Yield)

To eliminate emissivity dependence, a **ratio (two-color) pyrometer** measures brightness at two wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$:

$$R = \frac{B_{\lambda_1}}{B_{\lambda_2}} = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \exp\!\left[ -\frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \right]$$

For gray bodies, emissivity cancels out, giving an **emissivity-independent** temperature. This is a frequent **Part B-13 mark** KTU question.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Ready)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Validity Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B_\lambda = \dfrac{c_1}{\lambda^5 [\exp(c_2/\lambda T) - 1]}$ | Planck's law (exact) | Universal |
| 2 | $B_\lambda \approx \dfrac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp(-c_2/\lambda T)$ | Wien's approximation | $\lambda T \ll c_2$ |
| 3 | $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$ | Wien's displacement law | All $T$ |
| 4 | $E = \sigma T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (total) | Blackbody |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T_t} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$ | Emissivity correction | Optical pyrometer |
| 6 | $B_\lambda(T_t) = B_\lambda(T_f)$ | Brightness matching (null) | DFOP principle |
| 7 | $R = \dfrac{B_{\lambda_1}}{B_{\lambda_2}}$ | Two-color ratio | Ratio pyrometer |
| 8 | $\ln R = 5\ln(\lambda_2/\lambda_1) - \dfrac{c_2}{T}\left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2}\right)$ | Ratio temperature solve | Ratio pyrometer |
| 9 | $T_f = T_t \cdot \varepsilon_\lambda^{1/4}$ (approx for total) | Total radiation correction | Total pyrometer |

**Constants to memorize:**
- $c_1 = 3.7415 \times 10^{-16} \, \text{W·m}^2$
- $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$
- $\lambda_{\text{red}} = 0.65 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why Optical Pyrometer? |
|---|---|---|
| **Steel / Metals** | Molten metal in furnaces (1500–1800 °C) | Non-contact, survives hostile environments |
| **Glass manufacturing** | Gob temperature measurement | Fast response, no sensor contamination |
| **Cement kilns** | Burning-zone temperature | Continuous, remote-sensing possible |
| **Aerospace / Plasma** | Rocket exhaust, re-entry temperatures | Only non-contact method viable |
| **Semiconductor** | Wafer processing | Avoids contamination from probes |
| **Astronomy** | Stellar surface temperatures (3000–30000 K) | Direct application of Wien's law |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When answering "give two applications," mention at least one *industrial* (metals/glass) and one *scientific* (astronomy/space) — this shows breadth and earns full marks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Wien's Approximation from Planck's Law

**Starting point:** Planck's spectral radiance:

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \left[ \exp\!\left( \dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T} \right) - 1 \right]}$$

**Step 1 — Define the dimensionless parameter** $\alpha = \dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T}$. For optical pyrometry with $\lambda \approx 6.5 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{m}$ and $T \geq 1000 \, \text{K}$:

$$\alpha = \frac{0.014388}{6.5 \times 10^{-7} \times 1000} = \frac{0.014388}{0.00065} \approx 22.1$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that $\alpha \gg 1$**, so the term $\exp(\alpha)$ vastly dominates over $1$:

$$\exp(\alpha) - 1 \approx \exp(\alpha) \quad \text{when } \alpha \gg 1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back** into Planck's law:

$$B_\lambda(T) \approx \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5 \, \exp(c_2/\lambda T)}$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange** by moving the exponential to the numerator:

$$B_\lambda(T) \approx \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T} \right)$$

This is **Wien's approximation**. The validity condition is **$\lambda T < 0.003 \, \text{m·K}$**, comfortably satisfied in optical pyrometry.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Significance:** A derivation question may explicitly ask "justify Wien's approximation for optical pyrometers." You must show the **magnitude of $\alpha$** as numerical evidence — this is what differentiates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

## 3.2 Derivation: True Temperature from Brightness Temperature (Emissivity Correction)

**Starting condition:** Target has emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$; filament at brightness $T_f$ matches the target's monochromatic brightness $B_\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Express the actual spectral radiance of the target** (gray body approximation):

$$B_\lambda^{\text{target}} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Express the filament's radiance** (treated as a near-blackbody at temperature $T_f$):

$$B_\lambda^{\text{filament}} = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the brightness-matching null condition**:

$$B_\lambda^{\text{target}} = B_\lambda^{\text{filament}}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel common terms** $\dfrac{c_1}{\lambda^5}$ from both sides:

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} \right) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Take the natural logarithm** of both sides:

$$\ln \varepsilon_\lambda - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} = -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange** to isolate the $1/T_t$ term:

$$\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} = \frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f} + \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by $\lambda/c_2$**:

$$\frac{1}{T_t} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$$

This is the **master correction equation**.

**Step 8 — Solve for true temperature:**

$$\boxed{\;T_t = \dfrac{1}{\dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda}\;}$$

**Sanity check:** For $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$ (blackbody), $\ln \varepsilon_\lambda = 0$, hence $T_t = T_f$. ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):** An optical pyrometer fitted with a red filter of wavelength $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$ is sighted on a hot steel surface. The filament disappears when the dial reads $T_f = 1500 \, \text{K}$. If the emissivity of steel at this wavelength is $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.6$, calculate the true temperature $T_t$ of the steel surface.

**Given data:**
- $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $T_f = 1500 \, \text{K}$
- $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.6$
- $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the correction term:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.5176 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the natural logarithm:**

$$\ln \varepsilon_\lambda = \ln(0.6) = -0.5108$$

**Step 3 — Compute the correction product:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda = 4.5176 \times 10^{-5} \times (-0.5108) = -2.308 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $1/T_f$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{1500} = 6.6667 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Compute $1/T_t$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_t} = 6.6667 \times 10^{-4} + (-2.308 \times 10^{-5}) = 6.4359 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Invert to get $T_t$:**

$$T_t = \frac{1}{6.4359 \times 10^{-4}} = 1553.8 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to Celsius:**

$$T_t = 1553.8 - 273.15 \approx 1280.65 \, °C$$

**Answer:** True temperature $\approx 1554 \, \text{K}$ or $\approx 1281 \, °C$. The steel is *hotter* than the brightness temperature indicated by the pyrometer, as expected.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examination Strategy:** Always state **both units** (K and °C) and explicitly mention the **direction of the error** (brightness reading underestimates the true temperature for non-blackbodies).

## 3.4 Derivation: Two-Color Pyrometer Temperature

**Step 1 — Write Wien's brightness at two wavelengths:**

$$B_{\lambda_1}(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda_1^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda_1 T} \right)$$

$$B_{\lambda_2}(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda_2^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda_2 T} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

$$R = \frac{B_{\lambda_1}}{B_{\lambda_2}} = \left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right)^5 \exp\!\left[ -\frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Take natural logarithm:**

$$\ln R = 5 \ln\!\left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right) - \frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for T:**

$$\frac{c_2}{T}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1} - \frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) = 5 \ln\!\left(\frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1}\right) - \ln R$$

$$\boxed{\;T = \dfrac{c_2 \left( \dfrac{1}{\lambda_1} - \dfrac{1}{\lambda_2} \right)}{5 \ln(\lambda_2/\lambda_1) - \ln R}\;}$$

**For a gray body**, the emissivity $\varepsilon$ cancels out — proving ratio pyrometers are **emissivity-independent**.

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right Pyrometer

| Scenario | Recommended Pyrometer | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Molten steel at 1600 °C, view obstruction | **Optical (DFOP)** | Single-wavelength measurement through small sighting port |
| Polished metal surface, unknown emissivity | **Two-Color Pyrometer** | Ratio method eliminates emissivity dependence |
| Furnace wall, total heating flux needed | **Total Radiation Pyrometer** | Stefan-Boltzmann integrates over all $\lambda$ |
| Moving target (rotating kiln) | **Optical** | Fast response, no thermal lag |
| Target obscured by smoke/dust | **Two-Color or Infrared** | Narrow wavelength windows cut through smoke |
| Temperatures < 600 °C | **Thermocouple / RTD** | Pyrometers lack sensitivity below red glow |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The optical pyrometer's internal construction is conceptually mapped below. Each block represents a *function*, with signal/light flow arrows indicating the optical path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["TARGET HOT BODY"] --> B["OBJECTIVE LENS"]
    B --> C["RED NARROW BAND FILTER"]
    C --> D["REFERENCE TUNGSTEN FILAMENT"]
    D --> E["EYEPIECE WITH APERTURE"]
    E --> F["OBSERVER EYE"]
    G["RHEOSTAT POWER SUPPLY"] --> D
    D --> H["CALIBRATED DIAL OR DPM"]
    H --> I["TEMPERATURE READOUT K OR C"]

    subgraph OPTICAL_PATH["OPTICAL PATH LEFT TO RIGHT"]
        B
        C
        D
        E
    end

    subgraph ELECTRICAL_PATH["ELECTRICAL CONTROL LOOP"]
        G
        H
    end
```

### Block-by-Block Functional Explanation

| Block | Function | Engineering Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Hot Body** | The unknown incandescent source emitting broadband thermal radiation | Must be a *blackbody cavity* or have known $\varepsilon_\lambda$ |
| **Objective Lens** | Collects radiation and forms a sharp image of the target | Telescope-grade achromatic doublet, focal length 100–200 mm |
| **Red Narrow-Band Filter** | Restricts measurement to a single effective wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$ | Interference filter with bandwidth $\sim 10 \, \text{nm}$ |
| **Reference Tungsten Filament** | Calibrated comparison source; current-controlled brightness | Operates below sublimation point (~3000 K max) |
| **Eyepiece with Aperture** | Magnifies and limits field of view for matching | Aperture restricts to small target area (~1 mm²) |
| **Observer Eye** | Detects null condition (filament disappearance) | Photopic vision at 555 nm most sensitive |
| **Rheostat / Power Supply** | Adjusts filament current to vary its brightness | Stable DC source; calibrated against blackbody |
| **Calibrated Dial / DPM** | Reads temperature corresponding to current setting | Pre-calibrated against gold point (1063 °C) |
| **Temperature Readout** | Indicates measured temperature in K or °C | Analog dial or digital panel meter |
