# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** Define **optical pyrometer**. Why is it used for high-temperature measurements only?

**Model Answer:**

An **optical pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument that determines the temperature of an incandescent body by visually matching its monochromatic brightness with that of a calibrated reference filament at a single effective wavelength (typically 0.65 µm).

It is restricted to high temperatures because:

1. The technique relies on **visible-spectrum brightness comparison**, which requires the target to be incandescent (glowing red, orange, white). Below ~800 K, the target emits negligible visible radiation.
2. Wien's approximation used in the instrument is accurate only when **$\lambda T \ll c_2$**, a condition satisfied only at high temperatures.
3. Conventional contact sensors (thermocouples) fail at temperatures >1300 °C due to material degradation; optical pyrometers avoid this.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern (KTU):** Definition — 1 mark; any two reasons — 2 marks.

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** State **Wien's displacement law**. Mention its significance in optical pyrometry.

**Model Answer:**

**Wien's displacement law** states that the wavelength at which the blackbody spectral emissive power is maximum is inversely proportional to the absolute temperature:

$$\lambda_{\max} \, T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$$

**Significance in optical pyrometry:**

1. It justifies the choice of **red filter wavelength (~0.65 µm)** for matching the visible peak emission of targets in the 1000–2500 K range.
2. It enables **temperature estimation** from a single-wavelength intensity measurement, the fundamental premise of the optical pyrometer.
3. As temperature rises, $\lambda_{\max}$ shifts to shorter (bluer) wavelengths — explaining why hotter targets appear whiter/bluer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern (KTU):** Statement + formula — 1.5 marks; Significance — 1.5 marks.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Each Part B question provides two alternative questions, and the student must answer **one** of them. Each alternative carries sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Below we provide **two independent full-length alternatives** as required.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A — Full Model (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q:** (a) Derive the working equation of an optical pyrometer with the help of a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the **red filter** and the **reference filament**.  **(7 Marks)**

(b) A disappearing filament optical pyrometer is used to measure the temperature of a metal surface. The filament disappears at a brightness temperature of $T_f = 1450 \, \text{K}$. The pyrometer uses a red filter of wavelength $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$ and the metal has a spectral emissivity $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.55$ at this wavelength. Calculate the **true temperature** $T_t$ of the metal. Take $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$.  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Statement of principle (1 mark):**

An optical pyrometer works on the principle of **brightness matching** at a single effective wavelength using **Wien's radiation law**:

$$B_\lambda(T) = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Working equation derivation (4 marks):**

The brightness of the target (gray body) at its true temperature $T_t$ is:

$$B_\lambda^{\text{target}} = \varepsilon_\lambda \cdot \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} \right)$$

The reference filament is approximated as a blackbody at its operating temperature $T_f$:

$$B_\lambda^{\text{filament}} = \frac{c_1}{\lambda^5} \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f} \right)$$

At the null condition (filament disappears), the two brightnesses are equal:

$$B_\lambda^{\text{target}} = B_\lambda^{\text{filament}}$$

$$\varepsilon_\lambda \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} \right) = \exp\!\left( -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f} \right)$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln \varepsilon_\lambda - \frac{c_2}{\lambda T_t} = -\frac{c_2}{\lambda T_f}$$

Solving for $1/T_t$:

$$\frac{1}{T_t} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda$$

This is the **working equation** of an optical pyrometer. (For $\varepsilon_\lambda = 1$, we get $T_t = T_f$.)

**Step 3 — Block diagram and roles (2 marks):**

*(Refer to SECTION 4 Diagram for the schematic.)*

- **Red filter (λ ≈ 0.65 µm):** Restricts the comparison to a single effective wavelength. This is essential because the eye's response is wavelength-dependent, and Wien's law requires *monochromatic* brightness for a unique temperature determination. Without the filter, the colour difference between target and filament would prevent accurate matching.
- **Reference filament:** A tungsten wire whose temperature depends on the current passing through it. By varying the current (via a calibrated rheostat), the operator controls the filament's brightness. The current required for null matching is pre-calibrated against a laboratory blackbody, so the dial directly indicates temperature.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $T_f = 1450 \, \text{K}$
- $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$
- $\varepsilon_\lambda = 0.55$
- $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the correction factor $\lambda/c_2$:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.5176 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate $\ln \varepsilon_\lambda$:**

$$\ln(0.55) = -0.5978$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the correction term:**

$$\frac{\lambda}{c_2} \ln \varepsilon_\lambda = (4.5176 \times 10^{-5})(-0.5978) = -2.7006 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $1/T_f$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{1450} = 6.8966 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $1/T_t$:**

$$\frac{1}{T_t} = 6.8966 \times 10^{-4} + (-2.7006 \times 10^{-5}) = 6.6265 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate $T_t$:**

$$T_t = \frac{1}{6.6265 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1509.1 \, \text{K}$$

**Step 7 — Express in Celsius:**

$$T_t = 1509.1 - 273.15 \approx 1235.95 \, °C$$

**Final Answer:** $T_t \approx \mathbf{1509 \, K}$ or $\mathbf{1236 \, °C}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error:** Students often write $\dfrac{1}{T_t} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} - \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2}\ln\varepsilon_\lambda$. **This is wrong.** The correct form is $\dfrac{1}{T_t} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2}\ln\varepsilon_\lambda$ — the logarithm itself is negative for $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Keep $c_2$ in m·K and $\lambda$ in metres; if $\lambda$ is in µm, divide $c_2$ accordingly.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert to °C:** The question may ask for °C; always provide both.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B — Full Model (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 4]**

**Q:** (a) Compare **total radiation pyrometer**, **optical pyrometer**, and **two-color pyrometer** with respect to working principle, governing law, advantages, and limitations.  **(7 Marks)**

(b) Explain the construction and working of a **disappearing filament optical pyrometer** with a block diagram. Why is the **null method** preferred over absolute brightness measurement?  **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (a) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Feature | Total Radiation Pyrometer | Optical (Monochromatic) Pyrometer | Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Measures total radiant flux from target | Measures monochromatic brightness at single λ | Measures ratio of brightness at two λ |
| **Governing Law** | Stefan-Boltzmann: $E = \sigma T^4$ | Wien's approximation: $B_\lambda = (c_1/\lambda^5)\exp(-c_2/\lambda T)$ | Wien's ratio: $R = B_{\lambda_1}/B_{\lambda_2}$ |
| **Detector** | Thermopile or thermistor (broadband) | Human eye or photodetector (narrow band) | Two photodetectors with narrow filters |
| **Emissivity Dependence** | Yes (requires $\varepsilon$ correction) | Yes (requires $\varepsilon_\lambda$ correction) | **No** (cancels for gray bodies) |
| **Wavelength Range** | All wavelengths | Single (e.g., 0.65 µm) | Two (e.g., 0.65 µm and 0.55 µm) |
| **Advantages** | Simple, rugged, integrates over all $\lambda$ | Brightness matching → high accuracy; visual null method | Emissivity-independent; ideal for dusty/smoky environments |
| **Limitations** | Emissivity error; absorbs intervening gases | Emissivity error; requires visible glow | More complex; requires dual-beam optics |
| **Typical Range** | 500–3000 K | 800–6000 K | 1000–5000 K |
| **Example Use** | Furnace wall temperature | Molten steel, stellar temperature | Re-entry vehicles, plasma diagnostics |

**Step 2 — Conclusion (2 marks):**

The choice of pyrometer depends on the **temperature range**, **emissivity knowledge**, and **environmental conditions**. Optical pyrometers dominate above 1000 °C where visible glow is available. Two-color pyrometers are preferred when emissivity is uncertain or varying.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Construction (3 marks):**

The major components of a DFOP are:

1. **Objective lens:** A telescope-grade lens that collects radiation from the target and forms an image of the target in the plane of the reference filament.
2. **Red narrow-band filter:** An interference filter centered at $\lambda \approx 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$ (or a rotating red glass) inserted in the optical path to ensure monochromatic comparison.
3. **Reference filament:** A small tungsten wire (typically a hairpin or loop) mounted in the focal plane of the objective lens, illuminated from behind by a small lamp whose current is varied.
4. **Eyepiece:** Magnifies the combined image of filament and target for the observer's eye.
5. **Rheostat and calibrated dial:** Adjusts filament current; the dial reads the corresponding temperature.
6. **Power source:** A stable DC supply (battery) to drive the filament.

**Step 2 — Working (2 marks):**

*(Refer to SECTION 4 for the operating sequence.)* The target's image is formed with the filament superimposed. The operator adjusts the rheostat until the filament brightness matches the target's brightness. At null, the filament "disappears" — no contrast is visible. The dial reading gives the brightness temperature $T_f$, which is then corrected for emissivity.

**Step 3 — Why null method is preferred (2 marks):**

The null method is preferred because:

1. The human eye is **highly sensitive to small contrast differences** but poor at *absolute* brightness estimation. Null detection reduces subjective error.
2. Null condition is **independent of source intensity variations** — it only requires equal brightness, eliminating calibration drift.
3. It enables **automatic servo-controlled** versions, where a photodetector replaces the eye and a feedback loop drives the null — improving accuracy and allowing remote readout.
4. It is a **comparative measurement**, inherently more accurate than absolute measurement.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Skipping the calibration basis:** Many students forget to mention that the filament current is calibrated against a **gold-point blackbody** ($T = 1063 °C$) or other fixed points. Always cite the calibration source.
> 2. **Confusing "brightness temperature" with "true temperature":** Brightness temperature is what the pyrometer reads; true temperature is corrected via emissivity. Examiner's frequently deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 3. **Forgetting the red filter wavelength:** A common omission is failing to specify $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$ when explaining DFOP operation.

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Last-Night Revision (Print This Section):**

- ✅ Optical pyrometer measures **monochromatic brightness** of a glowing target against a calibrated filament.
- ✅ Operating principle: **brightness matching (null method)** using Wien's approximation.
- ✅ Governing equation: $\dfrac{1}{T_t} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{c_2}\ln\varepsilon_\lambda$.
- ✅ Wien's approximation: $B_\lambda(T) \approx \dfrac{c_1}{\lambda^5}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{c_2}{\lambda T}\right)$, valid when $\lambda T \ll c_2$.
- ✅ **Standard red filter wavelength:** $\lambda = 0.65 \, \mu\text{m}$.
- ✅ **Second radiation constant:** $c_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{m·K}$.
- ✅ **Calibration reference:** Gold point at $1063 \, °C$ ($1336.15 \, \text{K}$).
- ✅ Temperature range: **800 K to 6000 K**.
- ✅ **True temperature > brightness temperature** for non-blackbodies (since $\ln \varepsilon_\lambda < 0$).
- ✅ DFOP components: objective lens → red filter → reference filament → eyepiece → observer.
- ✅ **Two-color pyrometer advantage:** Emissivity-independent (ratio cancels $\varepsilon$).
- ✅ **Total radiation pyrometer** uses Stefan-Boltzmann law; absorbs all $\lambda$.
- ✅ Wien's displacement law: $\lambda_{\max} T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m·K}$.
- ✅ Applications: molten metals, glass, kilns, plasma, stellar astronomy.
- ✅ **Sign pitfall:** Always check that the emissivity correction increases the true temperature when $\varepsilon_\lambda < 1$.
- ✅ Two-color temperature formula: $T = \dfrac{c_2(1/\lambda_1 - 1/\lambda_2)}{5\ln(\lambda_2/\lambda_1) - \ln R}$.
- ✅ Total radiation correction: $T_t = T_f \cdot \varepsilon_\lambda^{1/4}$ (approximation only).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Always draw the block diagram from memory in the answer sheet. Examiners reward diagrams generously — a well-labelled DFOP block diagram is worth 2–3 marks even before the explanation begins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermistor, Pressure Measurement, Manometer of U-Tube
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT638 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Thermistor, Pressure Measurement, Manometer of U-Tube

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 — Process Sensors and Static Characteristics (Part B)

This module summary block addresses three foundational primary-sensing elements that recur in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE): the **Thermistor** (a thermal-resistive temperature sensor), the broader **Pressure Measurement** framework, and the classical **U-Tube Manometer** (a hydrostatic pressure standard). All three topics are interconnected because the manometer is frequently the *reference/transducer element* used to calibrate the very pressure gauges we study.

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Thermistor — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermistor (Thermal Resistor):** A two-terminal, solid-state, semiconductor-based passive sensor whose electrical resistance is a strong, repeatable, and typically non-linear function of its body temperature. Thermistors are manufactured from sintered metal-oxide compounds (e.g., **manganese, nickel, cobalt, iron oxides**) doped to give either a Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) or a Positive Temperature Coefficient (PTC).

In KTU 2024-scheme parlance, the thermistor is a *primary temperature transducer* that converts thermal energy into a measurable electrical resistance change, which is then conditioned by a Wheatstone bridge or a precision voltage divider.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Breath of the Semiconductor"
Imagine a classroom where students (charge carriers in the semiconductor) get more energetic when the room is warm. As the room (the thermistor bead) heats up, the students (electrons) collide and move faster, and **more** of them escape from the lattice, making it *easier* for current to flow — that is, resistance **drops**. This is exactly how an NTC thermistor behaves. For a PTC, imagine the students forming a chain (a crystalline phase change) as the room heats, **blocking** the hallway — resistance shoots up.

### 1.2 Pressure Measurement — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure:** The normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a real or imaginary surface. In instrumentation, "pressure measurement" is the act of converting this distributed fluid force into a quantifiable electrical or mechanical signal using a primary element (e.g., diaphragm, Bourdon tube, manometer, piezoelectric crystal).

The KTU syllabus emphasises pressure as a *process variable* in second-order importance only to temperature and flow, and it introduces the **U-Tube Manometer** as the *primary standard* against which all other pressure instruments are calibrated.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Drinking Straw"
Insert a straw into water, seal the top with your finger, and lift it. The water stays in the straw because atmospheric pressure pushes up from below to balance the weight of the water column inside. A manometer is essentially a *calibrated straw*: it converts an unknown pressure into a **measurable height of liquid** — converting an invisible force into a visible column.

### 1.3 U-Tube Manometer — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **U-Tube Manometer:** A differential pressure-measuring device consisting of a transparent tube bent into a 'U' shape, partially filled with a manometric fluid (e.g., **mercury, water, oil, carbon tetrachloride**). The two open (or tapped) ends are connected to the two points between which the pressure difference is to be measured. The deflection of the fluid column — called the *manometer deflection* or *manometer reading* `h` — is a direct, hydrostatic measure of the differential pressure.

#### Conceptual Analogy — "The Seesaw of Fluids"
Picture a playground seesaw with two children. If one child (pressure P₁) is heavier, the seesaw tilts toward that side, and the other child (pressure P₂) is lifted by a height `h`. The U-tube works exactly like this seesaw, except the *children* are mercury columns and the *height difference* is the visible indicator of which side's pressure is winning.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics and Constants (used throughout this module)

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.80665\ \text{m/s}^2$
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $1\ \text{atm} = 101325\ \text{Pa} = 760\ \text{mmHg}$ (at 0 °C)
- **Density of mercury at 0 °C:** $\rho_{Hg} \approx 13595\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of water at 4 °C:** $\rho_{H_2O} = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In numerical problems, always carry forward $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ unless the question explicitly says "at standard gravity". Mixing $g = 10$ in intermediate steps is a common mark-loss pitfall.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section systematically deconstructs each device — first the *operational logic*, then the *mathematical model* — and consolidates all board-relevant equations into the KTU Formula Sheet at the end of the section.

---

## 2.1 The Thermistor — Operating Principle, Types, and Characteristics

### 2.1.1 Operational Logic (NTC type, the KTU default)

1. The thermistor body is a *sintered oxide* semiconductor with a high density of charge-trap states.
2. As temperature `T` rises, the thermal energy $k_B T$ ionises more donor/acceptor states, releasing additional **free electrons or holes** into the conduction band.
3. The increased carrier density **lowers the bulk resistance** — a negative temperature coefficient.
4. The change in resistance is detected by a **Wheatstone bridge** (or a constant-current source) and converted to a voltage.
5. The voltage is then linearised, scaled, and displayed in °C (or K).

### 2.1.2 The Three Families of Thermistors

| Family | Symbol | Behavior | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| Negative Temperature Coefficient | **NTC** | Resistance falls as T rises | High-accuracy temperature sensing, inrush current limiters |
| Positive Temperature Coefficient | **PTC** | Resistance rises with T (steeply above Curie point) | Self-resetting fuses, motor protection |
| Critical Temperature Resistor | **CTR** | Resistance drops *abruptly* at a critical T | Solid-state temperature alarms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcomes for Module 4 require the student to *"explain the working principle of NTC and PTC thermistors with characteristic curves."* Memorise the qualitative difference in the $R$–$T$ graphs: NTC is *exponentially decreasing*, PTC is *gently rising with a sharp knee*.

### 2.1.3 Mathematical Model of an NTC Thermistor

The governing equation — the *KTU-mandatory* Steinhart-Hart / Beta-parameter model — is:

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = thermistor resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (in **Kelvin**)
- $R_0$ = nominal resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically **298.15 K = 25 °C**)
- $\beta$ = material constant (Beta value), typically between **3000 K and 5000 K** for common NTCs
- $T$ = absolute temperature of the thermistor body, in **K**

For a higher-fidelity model, the **Steinhart-Hart cubic** is used:

$$\frac{1}{T} = a + b\,\ln R_T + c\,(\ln R_T)^3$$

where `a`, `b`, `c` are the Steinhart-Hart coefficients fitted by the manufacturer for the specific device.

### 2.1.4 Sensitivity of an NTC Thermistor

The temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR), $\alpha$, is:

$$\alpha = \frac{1}{R_T}\,\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

For a typical NTC with $\beta = 4000\ \text{K}$ at $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$:

$$\alpha = -\frac{4000}{(298.15)^2} \approx -0.045\ \text{K}^{-1}\ \text{(i.e., } {-4.5\%}\ \text{per K)}$$

Compare this to a Platinum RTD (PT100), which has $\alpha_{Pt} \approx +0.00385\ \text{K}^{-1}$ — the thermistor is **roughly 10× more sensitive**, but at the cost of severe non-linearity.

### 2.1.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical**: 10 kΩ NTC at 25 °C probes for patient thermometry (0.1 °C accuracy).
- **Industrial**: Motor winding overtemperature protection (PTC "thermistor protectors" embedded in windings).
- **Automotive**: Engine coolant temperature (ECT) sensors in the ECU's closed-loop fuel map.
- **Consumer**: 3D-printer hot-end temperature control, battery-pack protection in Li-ion packs.

---

## 2.2 Pressure Measurement — Taxonomy and Static Behaviour

### 2.2.1 The Four Reference Pressures (KTU must-know)

| Reference | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Atmospheric | $P_{atm}$ | Pressure of the ambient air (≈ 101.325 kPa at sea level) |
| Absolute | $P_{abs}$ | Pressure measured with respect to a **perfect vacuum** (true zero) |
| Gauge | $P_{gauge}$ | Pressure measured with respect to atmospheric: $P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| Vacuum | $P_{vac}$ | Pressure **below** atmospheric, expressed as a *positive* number: $P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion identity (always in KTU derivations):**
> $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

### 2.2.2 Classification of Pressure Measuring Instruments

1. **Hydrostatic (Manometric)**: U-tube, well-type, inclined, inverted — convert pressure to a *liquid column height*.
2. **Elastic/Deformation type**: Bourdon tube, diaphragm, capsule, bellows — convert pressure to *mechanical displacement*.
3. **Electrical**: Strain gauge, capacitive, piezoelectric, piezoresistive, LVDT-based — convert pressure to *electrical signal*.
4. **Dead-weight tester**: A *primary standard* — converts known masses into pressure ($P = mg/A$).

### 2.2.3 Static Characteristics of Pressure Transducers

These mirror the generic transducer characteristics covered in Module 2 of PEFTT638, but applied to pressure:

- **Range & Span**: e.g., 0–10 bar with span 10 bar.
- **Accuracy, Repeatability, Hysteresis, Linearity, Resolution**.
- **Sensitivity (static)**: $S = \Delta \text{output} / \Delta \text{input}$ (V/bar, mV/kPa).
- **Static stiffness**: $k = \Delta P / \Delta x$ (Pa/m) of the elastic element.

---

## 2.3 U-Tube Manometer — Operating Principle, Equation, and Variants

### 2.3.1 Operating Logic

1. Two pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$ are tapped into the two limbs of the U-tube.
2. The manometric fluid (e.g., mercury) finds its own level such that the **hydrostatic equation is satisfied** at any horizontal level within the connected fluid.
3. The deflection `h` of the heavy fluid, combined with knowledge of the fluid densities, yields the differential pressure.

### 2.3.2 The Two Reference Cases

#### Case A — U-Tube Manometer with Both Limbs Exposed to Gases (Same Fluid Above Mercury on Both Sides)

The two gases (often with negligible density $\rho_g$ compared to $\rho_{Hg}$) sit above the mercury. The differential pressure is:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{g})\,g\,h$$

When $\rho_g \ll \rho_{Hg}$ (the usual assumption), this simplifies to:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h$$

#### Case B — U-Tube Manometer with a Sealing Liquid Different from the Process Fluid

A typical setup has mercury in the U-tube, water in the upper portion of the left limb (with pressure $P_1$ acting on the water surface), and a different process fluid on the right limb (with pressure $P_2$):

$$P_1 + \rho_{w}\,g\,h_w - \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h - \rho_{f}\,g\,h_f = P_2$$

This is the **general form** the KTU examiner prefers for 14-mark derivation questions.

### 2.3.3 Sensitivity of a U-Tube Manometer

Sensitivity is the **change in manometer reading per unit change in differential pressure**:

$$S_{U} = \frac{\partial h}{\partial (\Delta P)} = \frac{1}{(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_g)\,g}$$

To *increase* sensitivity (larger `h` for a given $\Delta P$), KTU conventions suggest using a **lighter manometric fluid** (e.g., oil instead of mercury) and a **smaller diameter tube** (to reduce the volume of liquid that has to be displaced).

### 2.3.4 Variants of the U-Tube

| Variant | Modification | Sensitivity gain |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Well-type (cistern)** | One limb is a large reservoir, other is a thin tube | $\times$ (area ratio), typically 5–10× |
| **Inclined-tube** | Tube is laid at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal | $1/\sin\theta$ (e.g., $\theta = 30° \Rightarrow 2\times$) |
| **Inverted U-tube** | Light fluid (e.g., oil) above heavier process fluid | Used for *differential* pressure of liquids |
| **Differential U-tube** | Two fluids of close density (e.g., water & oil) | Best for small $\Delta P$ |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit | Valid For |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $R_T = R_0\,\exp\!\big[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)\big]$ | Resistance–Temperature model | Ω, K | NTC thermistor |
| 2 | $\alpha = -\beta / T^2$ | Temperature coefficient of resistance | K⁻¹ | NTC thermistor |
| 3 | $1/T = a + b\ln R_T + c(\ln R_T)^3$ | Steinhart-Hart cubic | K | NTC, high accuracy |
| 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ | Absolute ↔ Gauge conversion | Pa | All pressure measurements |
| 5 | $P = \rho\,g\,h$ | Hydrostatic pressure (basic) | Pa | Static fluid column |
| 6 | $P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h$ | U-tube, both ends gas | Pa | U-tube manometer (basic) |
| 7 | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_1 - \rho_2)\,g\,h$ | U-tube, two liquids in manometer | Pa | Differential manometer |
| 8 | $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g\,h$ | U-tube with sealing liquid | Pa | General KTU form |
| 9 | $P = mg/A$ | Dead-weight tester primary standard | Pa | Calibration lab |
| 10 | $S = 1/(\rho_m - \rho_f)g$ | U-tube sensitivity | m/Pa | Manometer design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mandatory Mnemonic:** The three "R"s of a thermistor are **R₀, β, T** — keep them in Kelvin, never in Celsius, to avoid the 1-mark cut that examiners regularly apply when students mix units.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> **KTU Directive:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No intermediate step is skipped. The *Analytical Matrix* at the end cross-links each derivation to a typical KTU sub-question.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resistance-Temperature Relationship of an NTC Thermistor (Beta-Parameter Form)

**Starting premise:** The intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ of a semiconductor is governed by the Boltzmann-activated Arrhenius-type law:

$$n_i \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where $E_g$ is the semiconductor bandgap (eV) and $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

**Step 1 — Conductivity link:** The electrical conductivity of the doped oxide is $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p) \propto n_i$, so:

$$\sigma \propto \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Resistance link:** Resistance is inversely proportional to conductivity, $R = L / (\sigma A)$, hence:

$$R \propto \exp\!\left(+\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{\beta}{T}\right)$$

where we define the **Beta parameter** $\beta \equiv E_g / (2 k_B)$.

**Step 3 — Normalise at $T_0$:** Divide the equation at $T$ by the same equation at reference $T_0$ to eliminate the unknown proportionality constant:

$$\frac{R_T}{R_0} = \frac{\exp(\beta/T)}{\exp(\beta/T_0)} = \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Final form:**

$$R_T = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Numerical sanity check:** Let $R_0 = 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 298.15\ \text{K}$, $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$, and $T = 323.15\ \text{K}$ (i.e., 50 °C).

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{323.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}$$

$$= 0.0030946 - 0.0033540 = -0.0002594\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

$$\beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right) = 3950 \times (-0.0002594) = -1.0246$$

$$R_T = 10000 \times e^{-1.0246} = 10000 \times 0.3590 \approx 3590\ \Omega \approx 3.59\ \text{k}\Omega$$

**Result:** The NTC thermistor resistance dropped from 10 kΩ to ~3.59 kΩ when temperature rose from 25 °C to 50 °C — a 64 % drop over 25 K, confirming the high sensitivity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Temperature Coefficient of Resistance of an NTC

**Step 1 — Take the natural log of the Beta equation:**

$$\ln R_T = \ln R_0 + \beta\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:**

$$\frac{1}{R_T}\,\frac{dR_T}{dT} = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the temperature coefficient of resistance $\alpha$:**

$$\boxed{\alpha = -\frac{\beta}{T^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation at $T = 298.15\ \text{K}$ and $\beta = 3950\ \text{K}$:**

$$\alpha = -\frac{3950}{(298.15)^2} = -\frac{3950}{88893.4} \approx -0.0444\ \text{K}^{-1} = -4.44\%\ \text{per K}$$

**Significance:** For every 1 K rise, the resistance drops by ~4.44 %. Over a 10 K span, the resistance changes by ~44 %, which is enormous and is why the thermistor is preferred for narrow-range, high-sensitivity measurements.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — U-Tube Manometer Equation (General Case with Three Fluids)

Consider a U-tube manometer connected between two pressure taps. The left limb carries **Process Fluid A** (pressure $P_1$, density $\rho_A$) above **Mercury** (density $\rho_{Hg}$), and the right limb carries **Process Fluid B** (pressure $P_2$, density $\rho_B$) above Mercury. The mercury deflection is $h$, with the left mercury level *falling* by $h/2$ and the right rising by $h/2$ (assuming equal limb cross-sections). The left process fluid column of height $h_A$ and the right process fluid column of height $h_B$ sit above the mercury.

**Step 1 — Apply the principle of communicating vessels.** At any common horizontal level within the connected mercury, the pressure is identical when traced from either side.

**Step 2 — Trace from $P_1$ downward through Fluid A, then through the mercury to the bottom and up the right limb, finally emerging at $P_2$:**

$$P_1 + \rho_A\,g\,h_A - \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h - \rho_B\,g\,h_B = P_2$$

**Step 3 — Re-arrange for the differential pressure:**

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h + \rho_B\,g\,h_B - \rho_A\,g\,h_A$$

**Step 4 — Special-case simplification:** If both process fluids are gases with $\rho_A, \rho_B \ll \rho_{Hg}$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h$$

**Step 5 — Special-case (inverted U-tube with two liquids of close density):** If Fluid A is the heavy manometric fluid and Fluid B is a light sealing fluid of density $\rho_f$:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_A - \rho_f)\,g\,h$$

**Step 6 — Numerical example:** Two water pipes (negligible gas density) are connected to a mercury U-tube manometer. The mercury deflection reads $h = 250\ \text{mm} = 0.25\ \text{m}$.

$$\Delta P = \rho_{Hg}\,g\,h = 13595 \times 9.81 \times 0.25$$

$$\Delta P = 33341.7\ \text{Pa} \approx 33.34\ \text{kPa}$$

**Step 7 — In SI-derived units:**

$$\Delta P \approx 0.329\ \text{atm}\quad \text{or}\quad \Delta P \approx 4.83\ \text{psi}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Sensitivity of a U-Tube Manometer

**Step 1 — Begin with the basic manometer equation:**

$$\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g\,h$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $h$:**

$$h = \frac{\Delta P}{(\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate $h$ with respect to $\Delta P$:**

$$\frac{\partial h}{\partial (\Delta P)} = \frac{1}{(\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g}$$

**Step 4 — This derivative is the *static sensitivity* $S_U$:**

$$S_U = \frac{1}{(\rho_m - \rho_f)\,g}$$

**Step 5 — For a mercury-air U-tube ($\rho_m = 13595\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_f \approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$):**

$$S_U = \frac{1}{(13595 - 1.2) \times 9.81} = \frac{1}{133343.7} \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m/Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:** Every 1 Pa of differential pressure produces only 7.5 μm of mercury deflection. This is why **inclined-tube** and **well-type** manometers are preferred for low-pressure measurements — they boost effective sensitivity by a factor of 5–10×.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Question-to-Derivation Cross-Index

| KTU Sub-Question Pattern | Applicable Derivation | Marks Split (typical) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| "Derive the R–T relationship of an NTC thermistor" | Derivation 1 | 7 |
| "Calculate the resistance at 80 °C given R₀ and β" | Derivation 1 (numerical step 5) | 3–4 |
| "Find the temperature coefficient α" | Derivation 2 | 4 |
| "Derive the U-tube manometer equation" | Derivation 3 | 7 |
| "Calculate ΔP from a manometer reading" | Derivation 3 (numerical step 6) | 3–4 |
| "Compare sensitivity of U-tube vs inclined manometer" | Derivation 4 + design | 5–7 |
| "Convert gauge pressure to absolute" | Formula 4 | 2 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> **KTU Directive:** All diagrams are rendered in **Mermaid** (text-based flowchart format) and represent **functional block-level architecture** rather than photographic cross-sections. Each diagram is self-contained and contains no markdown/HTML inside node labels.

---

## 4.1 NTC Thermistor Signal Conditioning — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SENS[Process Temperature T] --> TH[NTC Thermistor Bead]
    TH --> R[R_T Value Changes]
    R --> BR[Wheatstone Bridge Excitation]
    BR --> AMP[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    AMP --> LIN[Linearisation Lookup Table]
    LIN --> DSP[Microcontroller ADC and Display]
    DSP --> OUT[Digital Temperature in Degree Celsius]

    style SENS fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style TH fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4500
    style BR fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
    style AMP fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
    style LIN fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21
    style DSP fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21
    style OUT fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
```

**Reading the diagram:** Process heat → NTC bead changes resistance → bridge converts ΔR to ΔV → instrumentation amplifier boosts → linearisation table corrects the exponential R–T curve → microcontroller displays the calibrated temperature.

---

## 4.2 U-Tube Manometer — Functional Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Pressure Tap 1 at Point A] --> L1[Left Limb Process Fluid Column]
    P2[Pressure Tap 2 at Point B] --> L2[Right Limb Process Fluid Column]
    L1 --> MERCURY[Mercury Pool in U-Bend]
    L2 --> MERCURY
    MERCURY --> DEFLECTION[Manometer Reading h in metres]
    DEFLECTION --> CALC[Apply Hydrostatic Equation]
    CALC --> RESULT[Differential Pressure Delta P in Pascal]

    style P1 fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style P2 fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style L1 fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#7D6608
    style L2 fill:#FFF3CD,stroke:#7D6608
    style MERCURY fill:#D5DBDB,stroke:#212F3D
    style DEFLECTION fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21
    style CALC fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
    style RESULT fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
```

**Reading the diagram:** Two pressure taps feed process-fluid columns into the U-bend, which contains a heavy manometric fluid (mercury). The manometer deflection `h` is then converted to differential pressure via the hydrostatic equation.

---

## 4.3 Pressure Measurement System — End-to-End Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Fluid Pressure] --> PE[Primary Element Diaphragm or Bourdon]
    PE --> SEC[Secondary Element Strain Gauge or LVDT]
    SEC --> SG[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    SG --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> CPU[Microcontroller or PID Controller]
    CPU --> DISP[Digital Display]
    CPU --> ACT[Actuator or Final Control Element]
    CPU --> FB[Feedback to Process]

    style P fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style PE fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4500
    style SEC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4500
    style SG fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
    style ADC fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
    style CPU fill:#FADBD8,stroke:#922B21
    style DISP fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style ACT fill:#F5B7B1,stroke:#641E16
    style FB fill:#FCF3CF,stroke:#7D6608
```

**Reading the diagram:** A complete pressure measurement loop — pressure acts on the primary element (e.g., diaphragm), which deforms; a secondary element (e.g., strain gauge) converts the deformation into an electrical signal, which is conditioned, digitised, displayed, and used in a closed-loop control action.

---

## 4.4 Comparative Decision Tree — Choosing a Pressure Instrument

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START[New Pressure Measurement Requirement] --> Q1{Pressure Range}
    Q1 -->|Very Low under 1 kPa| INCL[Inclined Manometer]
    Q1 -->|Low 1 to 100 kPa| WELL[Well Type Manometer]
    Q1 -->|Medium 0.1 to 10 MPa| BOUR[Bourdon Tube]
    Q1 -->|High above 10 MPa| STRAIN[Strain Gauge or Piezoelectric]
    Q1 -->|Vacuum Sub atmospheric| PIR[ Pirani or Diaphragm Gauge]

    INCL --> D1[Use Oil or Water as Manometric Fluid]
    WELL --> D2[Use Mercury with Large Reservoir]
    BOUR --> D3[C Shaped or Helical Tube Design]
    STRAIN --> D4[Use Four Arm Wheatstone Bridge]
    PIR --> D5[Use Thermal Conductivity or Diaphragm Capsule]

    style START fill:#E8F4FF,stroke:#1F4E79
    style D1 fill:#D4EFD0,stroke:#196F3D
```
