# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** 
**Differentiate between a flat diaphragm and a corrugated diaphragm. Mention any two advantages of a corrugated diaphragm over a flat one. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Flat Diaphragm | Corrugated Diaphragm |
|---|---|---|
| Surface profile | Smooth, plain | Concentric corrugations (ripples) |
| Deflection for given $P$ | Small | Much larger (3–6×) |
| Maximum stress | Concentrated at edge | Distributed over corrugation crests |
| Linearity | Limited | Improved |
| Typical application | High-frequency dynamic pressure | Static and quasi-static pressure measurement |

**Two Advantages of Corrugated Diaphragm:**

1. **Larger deflection per unit pressure** — increases sensitivity of the transducer.
2. **Lower peak bending stress** — prolongs fatigue life and allows higher over-pressure without permanent deformation.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the formula for maximum deflection of a flat circular diaphragm clamped at its edges, and list the factors on which it depends. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

$$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{3 P R^{4}}{16 E t^{3}} (1 - \nu^{2})}$$

The maximum deflection depends on:
1. **Applied pressure $P$** — directly proportional.
2. **Diaphragm radius $R$** — proportional to the **fourth power** of radius.
3. **Young's Modulus $E$** of the material — inversely proportional.
4. **Thickness $t$** — inversely proportional to the **cube** of thickness.
5. **Poisson's ratio $\nu$** of the material — appears through $(1 - \nu^{2})$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Notice that $R$ has the highest power (4). A small change in radius drastically changes the deflection. Similarly, $t$ has the cube power — making it the most effective parameter for tuning sensitivity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **maximum deflection of a flat circular diaphragm** clamped at its edges and subjected to uniform pressure on one side. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A flat circular diaphragm of **Stainless Steel 316** has the following specifications:
- Radius $R = 30 \text{ mm}$
- Thickness $t = 0.25 \text{ mm}$
- Young's Modulus $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$
- Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.30$
- Applied pressure $P = 200 \text{ kPa}$

Calculate the **(i)** maximum deflection and **(ii)** maximum bending stress at the center. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Assumptions:**
1. The diaphragm material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
2. Deflection is **small** compared to thickness ($w \ll t$), so linear small-deflection (Kirchhoff–Love) plate theory applies.
3. The diaphragm is **thin** ($t/R < 1/20$).
4. The plate is **initially flat** and **free from in-plane stresses**.
5. The edges are **rigidly clamped** (encastré).
6. The pressure is **uniformly distributed** over the entire surface.

**Governing equation:**

$$\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left\{ r \frac{d}{dr} \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dw}{dr} \right) \right] \right\} = \frac{P}{D}$$

where flexural rigidity $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$.

**Boundary conditions:**
- $w(R) = 0$ and $\left. \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right|_{r=R} = 0$ (clamped edge)
- $w(0)$ finite and $\left. \dfrac{dw}{dr} \right|_{r=0} = 0$ (regular center)

**Step-by-step integration (as shown in Section 3.1):**

After four integrations and applying boundary conditions:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{256 D} (R^{2} - r^{2})^{2}$$

**Maximum deflection at center** ($r = 0$):

$$w_{max} = \frac{P R^{4}}{256 D} = \frac{3 P R^{4}}{16 E t^{3}} (1 - \nu^{2}) \quad \blacksquare$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$R = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.030 \text{ m}$
$t = 0.25 \text{ mm} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
$E = 200 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$
$\nu = 0.30$
$P = 200 \times 10^{3} \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Maximum Deflection:**

$$w_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot (2 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (0.030)^{4}}{16 \cdot (2 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^{3}} \cdot (1 - 0.30^{2})$$

Numerator:

$$3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{5} \cdot 8.1 \times 10^{-7} = 0.486$$

$$(1 - 0.09) = 0.91$$

Effective numerator: $0.486 \times 0.91 = 0.4423$

Denominator:

$$16 \cdot 2 \times 10^{11} \cdot 1.5625 \times 10^{-11} = 16 \cdot 3.125 = 50.0$$

$$\boxed{w_{max} = \frac{0.4423}{50.0} = 8.85 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 8.85 \text{ mm}}$$

**(ii) Maximum Bending Stress:**

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 P R^{2}}{8 t^{2}} (1 - \nu^{2})$$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{3 \cdot (2 \times 10^{5}) \cdot (0.030)^{2}}{8 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-4})^{2}} \cdot 0.91$$

Numerator: $3 \cdot 2 \times 10^{5} \cdot 9 \times 10^{-4} = 540$
Denominator: $8 \cdot 6.25 \times 10^{-8} = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}$
$(1-\nu^2) = 0.91$

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{540 \cdot 0.91}{5.0 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{491.4}{5.0 \times 10^{-7}} = 9.83 \times 10^{8} \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} \approx 983 \text{ MPa}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a **corrugated diaphragm pressure sensor**. Discuss the role of corrugations in improving linearity and sensitivity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A **capacitive pressure sensor** uses a corrugated diaphragm of effective radius $R = 20 \text{ mm}$ and thickness $t = 0.15 \text{ mm}$. The diaphragm is made of Beryllium Copper ($E = 130 \text{ GPa}$, $\nu = 0.34$). The corrugation flexibility factor is $K_c = 4.5$. For an applied pressure of $P = 50 \text{ kPa}$, compute the **(i)** maximum deflection and **(ii)** the percentage increase in deflection compared to a flat diaphragm of identical geometry. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Corrugated Diaphragm Construction & Working (7 Marks)

**Construction:**
A corrugated diaphragm is a thin metallic disc (Beryllium Copper, Phosphor Bronze, or Stainless Steel) on which concentric circular grooves (corrugations) are pressed using a die. The corrugated disc is clamped at its rim between two flanges. The cavity on the lower side is either evacuated (absolute pressure sensor) or vented to a reference pressure (gauge or differential pressure sensor). A capacitive plate is positioned a small distance below the diaphragm, forming a parallel-plate capacitor whose gap changes with deflection.

**Working:**
1. Pressure $P$ enters the upper chamber and acts on the corrugated surface.
2. The corrugations deform, allowing the central region of the diaphragm to deflect toward the lower plate.
3. The reduction in gap $d$ changes the capacitance:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - w(r)}$$
4. This capacitance change is measured by an AC bridge or oscillator circuit and converted into a voltage proportional to pressure.
5. Because the diaphragm returns to its original position when pressure is removed, the system is repeatable.

**Role of Corrugations:**
- **Linearity improvement:** The non-linear stiffness characteristic of corrugations nearly cancels the inherent geometric non-linearity of a flat plate, giving an almost **linear $P$–$w$ relationship** over a wide range.
- **Sensitivity enhancement:** Corrugations lower the effective bending stiffness by a factor of $K_c \approx 3$ to $6$, so the same pressure produces a much larger deflection.
- **Stress relief:** Peak bending stress is reduced and distributed, allowing higher over-pressure capability.
- **Temperature stability:** Properly shaped corrugations reduce thermal-induced stresses.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
$R = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.020 \text{ m}$
$t = 0.15 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$
$E = 130 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$
$\nu = 0.34$
$K_c = 4.5$
$P = 50 \text{ kPa} = 5 \times 10^{4} \text{ Pa}$

**(i) Maximum Deflection of Corrugated Diaphragm:**

$$w_c = \frac{P R^{4}}{E t^{3}} \cdot K_c \cdot (1 - \nu^{2})$$

Compute $(1-\nu^2)$:

$$1 - (0.34)^2 = 1 - 0.1156 = 0.8844$$

Compute $R^{4} = (0.020)^{4} = 1.6 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}$
Compute $t^{3} = (1.5 \times 10^{-4})^{3} = 3.375 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^{3}$

So:

$$w_c = \frac{(5 \times 10^{4}) \cdot (1.6 \times 10^{-7})}{(1.3 \times 10^{11}) \cdot (3.375 \times 10^{-12})} \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.8844$$

Compute denominator:

$$1.3 \times 10^{11} \cdot 3.375 \times 10^{-12} = 0.43875$$

Compute fraction:

$$\frac{5 \times 10^{4} \cdot 1.6 \times 10^{-7}}{0.43875} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-3}}{0.43875} = 0.01823 \text{ m}$$

Multiply by $K_c \cdot (1-\nu^2)$:

$$w_c = 0.01823 \cdot 4.5 \cdot 0.8844 = 0.01823 \cdot 3.9798 = 0.0726 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{w_c \approx 72.6 \text{ mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Check:** A deflection of 72.6 mm is physically unrealistic because $w_{max}$ must be much smaller than $t = 0.15$ mm for the small-deflection theory to apply. **This indicates the design is invalid** — the diaphragm would bottom out. In a real exam, comment on the limitation.

**(ii) Deflection of Flat Diaphragm (same geometry):**

$$w_{flat} = \frac{3 P R^{4}}{16 E t^{3}} (1-\nu^{2}) = \frac{3}{16} \cdot \frac{P R^{4}}{E t^{3}} (1-\nu^{2})$$

$$w_{flat} = 0.1875 \cdot 0.01823 \cdot 0.8844 = 3.024 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} \approx 3.02 \text{ mm}$$

**Percentage Increase:**

$$\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{w_c - w_{flat}}{w_{flat}} \times 100\% = \frac{72.6 - 3.02}{3.02} \times 100\%$$

$$\% \text{ Increase} = 23.04 \times 100\% \approx \mathbf{2304\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A factor of $K_c = 4.5$ means the corrugated version deflects $\approx 24\times$ more than the flat version. This is consistent with corrugation theory, but real designs must always verify that $w_{max} < 0.3 t$ for small-deflection theory to remain valid.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost KTU Students 2–4 Marks Each:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $(1-\nu^{2})$ term** in the deflection/stress formula — KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Mixing up** tangential stress at center vs. radial stress at edge — they have different magnitudes.
> 3. **Wrong units** — students commonly use $E$ in MPa but expect results in N/m². Always convert to **Pa**.
> 4. **Ignoring boundary conditions** — stating $w(0) = 0$ instead of $w(0) = w_{max}$.
> 5. **Not commenting on small-deflection validity** — if $w_{max} > 0.3 t$, mark down for not stating theory breakdown.
> 6. **Confusing** "stiffness" with "sensitivity" — corrugated = lower stiffness = higher sensitivity.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Diaphragm = primary elastic element** that converts pressure into mechanical deflection.
- [x] **Flat diaphragm:** simple disc, clamped edges, used in dynamic / high-frequency applications.
- [x] **Corrugated diaphragm:** disc with concentric ripples, used in static pressure sensors with high sensitivity.
- [x] **Maximum deflection formula (flat):** $w_{max} = \dfrac{3 P R^{4}}{16 E t^{3}} (1-\nu^{2})$
- [x] **Maximum stress formula (flat):** $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{3 P R^{2}}{8 t^{2}} (1-\nu^{2})$
- [x] **Deflection sensitivity** scales with $R^{4}$ and $t^{-3}$ — most powerful tuning knobs.
- [x] **Corrugations** reduce effective stiffness by a factor $K_c$ (typically 3–6), increase linearity, and redistribute stress.
- [x] **Boundary conditions for clamped edge:** $w(R)=0$ AND $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_R = 0$.
- [x] **Boundary condition at center (by symmetry):** $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\bigg|_0 = 0$, and $w(0) = w_{max}$.
- [x] **Small-deflection theory valid only if** $w_{max} < 0.3 t$.
- [x] **Flexural rigidity** $D = \dfrac{E t^{3}}{12(1-\nu^{2})}$ — appears in **all** plate equations.
- [x] **Industry preference:** Corrugated diaphragms dominate in modern pressure transmitters (Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJA).
- [x] **Flat diaphragms dominate** in dynamic pressure pickups (piezoelectric, blast pressure measurement).
- [x] **Material selection:** Beryllium Copper, Phosphor Bronze, SS 316, Inconel, Hastelloy for corrosive media.
- [x] **KTU ESE commonly tests:** formula derivation (10-mark or 7-mark sub-question) + numerical problem (7-mark sub-question).

---

**End of Module 4 — Flat and Corrugated Diaphragm Notes**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme — Process Instrumentation and Control (PEFTT638)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capsules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT638 - PROCESS INSTRUMENTATION AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Capsules ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **capsule** is a solid pharmaceutical dosage form in which the drug substance(s) and pharmaceutical excipients are enclosed within a small, sealed shell — most commonly made of **gelatin**, **HPMC (hydroxypropyl methylcellulose)**, or **starch derivatives**. The shell is generally designed to dissolve rapidly in the gastrointestinal tract, releasing the active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) for systemic absorption. In the context of **Process Instrumentation and Control (PEFTT638)**, the term *capsules* extends beyond the dosage form itself to the **entire instrumented manufacturing line** — encompassing capsule shell fabrication, powder/granule filling, sealing, sorting, polishing, weight verification, and quality assurance through closed-loop feedback control. There are two principal pharmaceutical categories: 1. **Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGCs)** — Two-piece (body + cap), rigid shells produced industrially by dipping stainless-steel pins into a gelatin solution. 2. **Soft Gelatin Capsules (SGCs)** — One-piece hermetically sealed shells formed by the **rotary die process**, encapsulating liquids, suspensions, or pastes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 emphasizes the *instrumentation* layer — i.e., how sensors, transducers, PLCs, and SCADA systems monitor and regulate each unit operation of capsule production. The dosage form itself is context; the *control architecture* is the examinable content. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are baking cookies at home. You prepare the dough (active ingredient + excipients), shape it, and bake it — but you must constantly check the oven temperature, weigh each cookie, and remove any burnt ones. **Capsule manufacturing is exactly this workflow** — but on an industrial scale, where human judgment is replaced by **temperature transmitters (TT)**, **load cells (weighing instruments)**, **vision inspection cameras**, and **PLC-based sorting actuators**. Think of each capsule as a *tiny, sealed envelope*. The envelope (shell) is made automatically by dipping molds into a hot liquid (gelatin). The letter inside (powder fill) is dosed with sub-milligram precision. Then a *postman* (the sorting system) checks every envelope's weight — too light means an empty letter, too heavy means two letters were stuffed in. The postman discards both. This entire conveyor belt of operations is what the **Process Instrumentation and Control** engineer designs, instruments, and tunes. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics The following physical constants and regulatory benchmarks govern capsule manufacturing instrumentation: - **Standard capsule sizes:** **0 (≈ 0.68 mL)**, **00 (≈ 0.95 mL)**, **1 (≈ 0.50 mL)**, **2 (≈ 0.37 mL)**, **3 (≈ 0.30 mL)**, **4 (≈ 0.21 mL)**. - **Drying tunnel temperature:** **22 °C – 28 °C** with relative humidity **30 % – 45 %**. - **Fill weight tolerance (USP <905>):** **±10 %** of the average fill weight for capsules <300 mg; **±7.5 %** for ≥300 mg. - **Gelatin viscosity (bloom strength):** **150 – 250 g** (standard); **pharmaceutical grade ≥ 200 g**. - **Drying time (HGCs):** typically **30 – 60 minutes** in a tunnel dryer. - **Standard fill speed:** modern high-speed machines operate at **up to 150,000 capsules/hour**. > [!NOTE] > These metrics are not arbitrary — they are codified in the **United States Pharmacopeia (USP)**, **Indian Pharmacopoeia (IP)**, and **European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.)**, all of which are referenced in KTU board questions. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Unit Operations in Capsule Manufacturing — Instrumentation Mapping Capsule production is decomposed into the following discrete unit operations. Each operation demands a distinct set of sensors, controllers, and actuators. The KTU examiner typically asks students to **map the instrumentation** for one or more of these operations. #### 2.1.1 Shell Fabrication (Hard Gelatin Capsules) 1. **Gelatin Melt Preparation** - *Why:* Gelatin must be liquefied at **55 °C – 65 °C** to allow uniform dipping. - *How:* A jacketed stainless-steel vessel with steam-heating is fitted with a **PT100 RTD temperature sensor** connected to a **PID temperature controller (TC)**. The controller modulates a **pneumatic control valve (TV)** on the steam line to maintain setpoint ±1 °C. - *Key parameter:* Viscosity (monitored via **inline viscometer**); bloom strength (offline check). 2. **Dip-Coating on Stainless Steel Pins** - *Why:* Capsule wall thickness is determined by the dwell time of pins in the gelatin bath and by withdrawal speed. - *How:* Pin immersion depth and withdrawal velocity are regulated by **servo motors** driven by a **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller)**. Pin speed is governed by the equation: $$v_{\text{pin}} = \frac{L}{t_{\text{withdraw}}}$$ where $v_{\text{pin}}$ is the withdrawal speed (m/s), $L$ is the gelatin film thickness dipped, and $t_{\text{withdraw}}$ is the dwell time. 3. **Drying Tunnel** - *Why:* Gelatin films must be dried uniformly to avoid tackiness and dimensional instability. - *How:* A multi-zone conveyor dryer is instrumented with: - **Humidity transmitters (HT)** in each zone. - **Temperature transmitters (TT)** controlling electric heaters or steam coils. - **Air velocity probes** to ensure uniform airflow. - The PLC implements **cascade control** (master = zone temperature, slave = heater output) for tight regulation. #### 2.1.2 Filling Operation 1. **Dosing Principle (Dosator / Auger / Vacuum):** - **Auger filling:** Powder is metered by the revolutions of an auger screw. Speed (RPM) directly correlates with fill weight. - **Dosator filling:** A piston-cylinder system pulls a precise volume of powder into a cavity before ejection into the capsule body. - **Tamping filling:** Powder is compacted into a slug by a series of tamping pins before being transferred into the capsule body. 2. **Closed-Loop Weight Control:** - The most critical instrumentation is the **checkweigher** (in-line load cell) positioned downstream of the filling station. Load cell output (mV/V) is amplified, digitized, and fed back to the PLC, which adjusts auger RPM or dosator stroke in real time. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **feedback equation** for fill weight control is: $$W_{\text{fill}}(t) = W_{\text{setpoint}} + K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ This is the classical **PID correction** applied to the dosing actuator. The error $e(t) = W_{\text{setpoint}} - W_{\text{measured}}(t)$ drives the dosing mechanism. #### 2.1.3 Sealing, Sorting, and Polishing - **Hard capsules (two-piece):** The cap is mechanically pressed onto the body. No sealing agent is required. - **Soft capsules:** Hermetic sealing is achieved by **thermal-wedge bonding** at the **rotary die** interface — instrumented with **thermocouples** to maintain die surface temperature within **35 °C – 45 °C**. - **Sorting:** Capsule orientation (cap-up vs. cap-down) is detected by a **photoelectric sensor** or **vision camera**; a **pneumatic jet actuator** rejects non-conforming units. - **Polishing:** A brush-and-vacuum system removes external powder residue; vacuum pressure is monitored by a **differential pressure transmitter (PDT)**. #### 2.1.4 Quality Verification (At-Line & In-Line) | Parameter | Instrument | Control Strategy | |---|---|---| | Fill weight | Load cell (checkweigher) | Closed-loop PID | | Disintegration | Disintegration tester (USP) | Statistical batch sampling | | Moisture content | NIR / Karl Fischer | At-line QC | | Hardness / friability | Hardness tester | At-line QC | | Visual defects | Vision inspection system | Rejector-actuator feedback | ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Application Context | Units | |---|---|---|---| | $Q = K \cdot A \cdot \Delta P$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow, $K$ = orifice constant, $A$ = area, $\Delta P$ = pressure drop | Bernoulli-based flow control in pneumatic capsule transport | m³/s | | $\tau = \frac{\mu \cdot \dot{\gamma}}{G}$ | $\tau$ = shear stress, $\mu$ = viscosity, $\dot{\gamma}$ = shear rate, $G$ = gelatin modulus | Gelatin rheology in capsule shell formation | Pa | | $e(t) = W_{\text{sp}} - W_{\text{meas}}$ | Error signal for fill weight PID | Dosing control loop | mg | | $v_{\text{pin}} = L / t_{\text{withdraw}}$ | Pin withdrawal velocity | Dip-coating dynamics | m/s | | $R_{\text{fill}} = \frac{W_{\text{actual}}}{W_{\text{target}}} \times 100\%$ | Fill weight ratio (% of target) | USP <905> compliance check | % | | $\Delta T = T_{\text{gel}} - T_{\text{pin}}$ | Gelatin-pintermperature differential | Thermal control of dipping bath | °C | | $H_{\text{cap}} = f(V_{\text{body}}, \rho_{\text{powder}})$ | Cap fill capacity as function of body volume and powder bulk density | Capsule size selection | mL | | $G(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller transfer function | Laplace-domain representation of dosing controller | — | > [!WARNING] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When writing the PID equation, students often confuse *controller output* with *error signal*. The controller output $u(t)$ is what drives the actuator, while the error $e(t)$ is the input to the controller. Examiners explicitly deduct marks for this confusion. ### 2.3 Engineering Utility The instrumentation architecture for capsule manufacturing finds direct analogy in: - **Continuous pharmaceutical manufacturing (CMAs)** — FDA-endorsed real-time release (RTR) testing. - **PAT (Process Analytical Technology)** frameworks — where NIR, Raman, and FTIR probes feed multivariate control loops. - **Industry 4.0 / Pharma 4.0** — where OPC-UA protocols interconnect filling, weighing, and vision systems on a single MES (Manufacturing Execution System) backbone. > [!NOTE] > In the KTU 2024 Scheme, questions on *PAT* and *Pharma 4.0* are emerging — students should be prepared to write 2–3 mark short notes on how capsule filling lines are integrated with MES/SCADA layers. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Closed-Loop PID Weight Control for Capsule Filling **Problem Setup:** A pharmaceutical company uses an auger-based capsule filling machine. The target fill weight is $W_{\text{sp}} = 250$ mg. The checkweigher's load cell measures the actual fill weight $W_{\text{meas}}(t)$ every 50 ms. A PLC implements a discrete PID controller to adjust the auger RPM. **Objective:** Derive the controller output equation in both continuous-time and discrete-time (z-domain) forms, suitable for KTU 14-mark analytical questions. #### Step 1 — Define the Error Signal The instantaneous error between the setpoint and the measured weight is given by: $$e(t) = W_{\text{sp}} - W_{\text{meas}}(t)$$ Substituting the numerical values: $$e(t) = 250 \text{ mg} - W_{\text{meas}}(t)$$ This is the *driving signal* of the controller. A positive error indicates the capsule is *underfilled*, so the auger RPM must increase. #### Step 2 — Apply the Continuous-Time PID Equation The classical parallel-form PID controller output is: $$u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t) + K_i \int_{0}^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$ where: - $K_p$ = proportional gain (dimensionless or in RPM/mg) - $K_i$ = integral gain (in RPM/(mg·s)) - $K_d$ = derivative gain (in RPM·s/mg) - $u(t)$ = controller output, interpreted as *auger RPM setpoint* #### Step 3 — Laplace Transform To analyze stability and tune the controller, we transform to the s-domain: $$U(s) = K_p E(s) + \frac{K_i}{s} E(s) + K_d s E(s)$$ Factoring $E(s)$: $$U(s) = \left(K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s\right) E(s)$$ Therefore, the **controller transfer function** is: $$G_c(s) = \frac{U(s)}{E(s)} = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$$ This is the canonical PID transfer function referenced in KTU formula sheets. #### Step 4 — Discrete-Time (Z-Transform) Realization Since PLCs operate in discrete time with a sampling period $T_s = 50$ ms $= 0.05$ s, the integral and derivative must be approximated: - **Integral approximation (backward Euler):** $$\int e(\tau)\,d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k)$$ - **Derivative approximation (backward difference):** $$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s}$$ Substituting into the PID equation: $$u(n) = K_p e(n) + K_i T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e(k) + K_d \frac{e(n) - e(n-1)}{T_s}$$ This is the **position-form** discrete PID algorithm. An equivalent **velocity-form** avoids integral windup: $$\Delta u(n) = K_p [e(n) - e(n-1)] + K_i T_s e(n) + \frac{K_d}{T_s} [e(n) - 2e(n-1) + e(n-2)]$$ #### Step 5 — Tuning (Ziegler-Nichols Closed-Loop Method) 1. Set $K_i = 0$ and $K_d = 0$. 2. Increase $K_p$ until sustained oscillations occur; record the *ultimate gain* $K_u$ and oscillation period $T_u$. 3. Apply Ziegler-Nichols rules for a PID controller: $$K_p = 0.6 \, K_u, \quad K_i = \frac{2 K_p}{T_u}, \quad K_d = \frac{K_p T_u}{8}$$ For a typical capsule filling line, $K_u \approx 4.5$ and $T_u \approx 1.2$ s, yielding: $$K_p \approx 2.7, \quad K_i \approx 4.5 \, \text{s}^{-1}, \quad K_d \approx 0.405 \, \text{s}$$ #### Step 6 — Stability Check (Routh-Hurwitz Simplified) For a first-order plant $G_p(s) = \frac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with $\tau_p = 0.3$ s (typical auger dynamics), the closed-loop characteristic equation is: $$1 + G_c(s) G_p(s) = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow (K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i)(\tau_p s + 1) + s = 0$$ Substituting the numerical gains: $$\Rightarrow 0.1215 s^3 + (1.215 + 0.81) s^2 + (2.7 + 0.81) s + 4.5 = 0$$ $$\Rightarrow 0.1215 s^3 + 2.025 s^2 + 3.51 s + 4.5 = 0$$ By Routh-Hurwitz, all coefficients are positive; the first-column elements yield: $$b_1 = \frac{2.025 \cdot 3.51 - 0.1215 \cdot 4.5}{2.025} \approx 3.245 > 0$$ Hence, **the closed-loop system is stable**. > [!IMPORTANT] > This derivation is the *exact analytical skeleton* expected in a KTU 14-mark Part B question. Marks are awarded for: (i) correct error definition (1 mark), (ii) Laplace transform (2 marks), (iii) discrete approximation (3 marks), (iv) tuning rule application (2 marks), and (v) stability verification (2 marks). The remaining 4 marks are for schematic, units, and conclusion. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sensor–Actuator Mapping for Capsule Line | Unit Operation | Primary Sensor | Transmitter | Controller | Actuator | Controlled Variable | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Gelatin melt tank | PT100 RTD | TT (4–20 mA) | PID temperature controller | Steam control valve | Melt temperature (55–65 °C) | | Dip-coating bath | Capacitive level sensor | LT | PLC analog input | Solenoid valve | Gelatin level | | Drying tunnel | Capacitive RH sensor + RTD | HT + TT | PLC + cascade PID | Electric heater + fan VFD | Tunnel T and RH | | Filling station (auger) | Incremental encoder | Tachometer | PLC motion controller | Servo drive on auger | Auger RPM | | Checkweigher | Strain-gauge load cell | WT (mV/V → 4–20 mA) | PLC discrete PID | Auger RPM setpoint | Fill weight (mg) | | Sorting vision system | CMOS camera | Vision processor | PLC | Pneumatic jet | Capsule orientation | | Softgel die | Thermocouple (J-type) | TT | PID | Die heater cartridge | Die surface T (35–45 °C) | | Polishing chamber | Differential pressure sensor | PDT | PLC | Vacuum blower VFD | Chamber vacuum (mbar) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hard Gelatin Capsule Manufacturing Line with Instrumentation ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["GELATIN MELT TANK
PT100 RTD + PID TC"] nodeB["DIPPING BATH
Capacitive Level Sensor"] nodeC["STAINLESS STEEL PINS
Servo Motor + Encoder"] nodeD["DRYING TUNNEL
RH Sensor + Temp Tx"] nodeE["PIN STRIPPER +
TRIMMING STATION"] nodeF["EMPTY CAPSULE
HOPPER + ORIENTER"] nodeG["FILLING STATION
Auger / Dosator"] nodeH["CHECKWEIGHER
Load Cell + PLC PID"] nodeI["CAP CLOSURE
Pneumatic Press"] nodeJ["VISION SORTER
CMOS Camera + Rejector"] nodeK["POLISHING CHAMBER
DP Transmitter"] nodeL["FINAL BLISTER PACK
SCADA OUTPUT"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ nodeJ --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL nodeH -. Feedback .-> nodeG nodeJ -. Reject Signal .-> nodeA nodeL -. MES Data .-> nodeA ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Control Loop Architecture ```mermaid graph LR sp["SETPOINT
Wsp = 250 mg"] sum["SUMMING JUNCTION
e(t) = Wsp - Wmeas"] ctrl["PID CONTROLLER
Kp Ki Kd"] act["ACTUATOR
Auger Servo Drive"] plant["PLANT
Auger + Capsule"] fb["FEEDBACK
Load Cell WT"] scada["SCADA HMI
Trend + Alarm"] sp --> sum sum -->|e(t)| ctrl ctrl -->|u(t)| act act --> plant plant -->|Wmeas| fb fb --> sum ctrl -. PV/SV .-> scada plant -. PV .-> scada ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Softgel Rotary Die Process Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["GELATIN MELT
60 C PT100"] nodeB["SPREADER BOX
Film Thickness 0.5 - 1.0 mm"] nodeC["COOLING DRUM
Chilled Roller 15 C"] nodeD["ROTARY DIES
J-type Thermocouple"] nodeE["WEDGE INJECTION
Liquid Filler Pump"] nodeF["SEALING ZONE
35 - 45 C"] nodeG["TRIMMER + WASHER
DP Sensor"] nodeH["DRIER + INSPECTOR
Vision + NIR"] nodeI["SORTER + PACKER
PLC Rejector"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeE --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG nodeG --> nodeH nodeH --> nodeI ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q1.** Differentiate between hard gelatin capsules and soft gelatin capsules with respect to their manufacturing processes and instrumentation requirements. **Model Answer:** | Feature | Hard Gelatin Capsules (HGCs) | Soft Gelatin Capsules (SGCs) | |---|---|---| | Shell structure | Two-piece (body + cap) | One-piece, hermetically sealed | | Manufacturing process | Dip-coating of steel pins → drying → stripping → joining | Rotary die process with simultaneous fill and seal | | Typical fill material | Powders, granules, pellets | Liquids, suspensions, pastes, oils | | Key temperature control | Gelatin bath: 55–65 °C | Die surface: 35–45 °C; Cooling drum: 12–18 °C | | Speed (modern lines) | Up to 150,000 caps/hr | Up to 30,000 caps/hr | | Hermetic seal | Mechanical (cap press) | Thermal wedge bond | | Instrumentation complexity | High (multi-zone dryer, vision, checkweigh) | Higher (rotary die synchronization, wedge temp.) | In summary, **HGCs** prioritize powder-dosing precision and high-throughput sorting, while **SGCs** require precise thermal sealing and synchronized fill injection, demanding tighter real-time control loops. --- **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q2.** Explain the role of a **checkweigher** with a load cell in a capsule filling line. How is its output used in closed-loop control? **Model Answer:** A **checkweigher** is an in-line weighing instrument that uses a **strain-gauge load cell** to measure the mass of each filled capsule as it travels along the conveyor. The load cell produces an analog voltage (typically 2–3 mV/V at full scale), which is amplified and digitized by a transmitter (WT) and fed to a **PLC** as a 4–20 mA or digital signal. In **closed-loop control**, the measured weight $W_{\text{meas}}(t)$ is continuously compared to the setpoint $W_{\text{sp}}$. The error $e(t) = W_{\text{sp}} - W_{\text{meas}}(t)$ drives a **PID controller** that adjusts the **auger RPM** or **dosator stroke** of the filling machine in real time. This compensates for variations in powder bulk density, flow rate, and machine drift, ensuring USP <905> weight uniformity. Additionally, capsules deviating beyond ±10 % are rejected via a downstream pneumatic actuator. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Describe the manufacturing process of hard gelatin capsules with a neat block diagram. Identify at least **six** instrumentation points and explain the control strategy for each. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The manufacturing of HGCs proceeds through the following sequence of unit operations: 1. **Gelatin melt preparation:** Gelatin powder is mixed with water, plasticizers (glycerin, sorbitol), and colorants in a jacketed stainless-steel vessel at 55–65 °C. The temperature is monitored by a **PT100 RTD** whose output is fed to a **PID temperature controller** that modulates a **pneumatic steam control valve (TV)**. Viscosity is checked offline via a **Brookfield viscometer**; the operator adjusts plasticizer concentration to maintain a bloom strength of 200–250 g. 2. **Dipping:** Stainless-steel pins (machined to capsule shape) are dipped into the gelatin bath. **Servo motors** control immersion depth and withdrawal velocity. A **PLC** governs the pin indexing mechanism through **incremental encoders** providing position feedback. 3. **Drying:** The gelatin-coated pins travel through a multi-zone conveyor dryer. Each zone has independent **temperature transmitters (TT)** and **humidity transmitters (HT)**. **Cascade control** is employed: the master loop sets zone temperature, and the slave loop controls heater duty cycle or steam valve position. 4. **Stripping and trimming:** Dried shells are stripped from the pins by bronze stripping jaws. Length is trimmed to specification via **laser optical gauges**. Off-spec shells are diverted by a **solenoid-actuated diverter**. 5. **Joining (body + cap):** Caps and bodies are oriented, joined, and inspected for proper fit. A **vision camera** verifies alignment; defective units are rejected pneumatically. 6. **Filling and checkweighing:** As discussed above, a **load cell** in the checkweigher closes the loop with the **auger servo drive**. 7. **Polishing and packaging:** External dust is removed via a brushed-vacuum system instrumented with a **differential pressure transmitter (PDT)**. | # | Instrumentation Point | Sensor | Controller | Actuator | Controlled Variable | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Gelatin bath | PT100 | PID TC | Steam CV | Melt T | | 2 | Dipping depth | LVDT | PLC | Servo motor | Pin immersion | | 3 | Dryer zone 1 | RH + T sensor | Cascade PID | Heater + fan VFD | Air T, RH | | 4 | Trim length | Laser gauge | PLC | Cutter motor | Shell length | | 5 | Checkweigh | Load cell | PID PLC | Auger servo | Fill weight | | 6 | Vision sort | CMOS camera | PLC | Pneumatic ejector | Orientation | **(b)** A capsule filling machine uses a dosator-type filling mechanism. The desired fill weight is **200 mg**. A step change in powder bulk density from **0.45 g/mL to 0.50 g/mL** is observed. Using the relation $W = \rho \cdot V$, calculate the change in fill volume required to maintain constant mass, and explain how the PLC adjusts the dosator stroke length. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Given the fill equation: $$W = \rho \cdot V$$ For constant fill mass $W = 200$ mg $= 0.200$ g, the dosator volume must satisfy: $$V = \frac{W}{\rho}$$ **Step 1 — Calculate the original dosator volume:** $$V_1 = \frac{0.200 \text{ g}}{0.45 \text{ g/mL}} = 0.4444 \text{ mL}$$ **Step 2 — Calculate the new required dosator volume:** $$V_2 = \frac{0.200 \text{ g}}{0.50 \text{ g/mL}} = 0.4000 \text{ mL}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the change in volume:** $$\Delta V = V_2 - V_1 = 0.4000 - 0.4444 = -0.0444 \text{ mL}$$ $$\Delta V \approx -44.4 \, \mu\text{L}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the result:** Since the powder bulk density *increased* (powder became denser), the same mass occupies a *smaller* volume. Hence, the dosator stroke length must *decrease* by an amount proportional to $\Delta V$. If the dosator bore diameter is $D = 6$ mm, the cross-sectional area is: $$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.006)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$ The required stroke-length adjustment is: $$\Delta L = \frac{\Delta V}{A} = \frac{-4.44 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3}{2.827 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2} = -1.571 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$ $$\Delta L \approx -1.57 \text{ mm}$$ **Step 5 — PLC adjustment:** The PLC receives the load cell reading (which would have spiked above 200 mg) and applies the PID algorithm to reduce the dosator piston stroke by approximately 1.57 mm. Since the dosator is driven by a **servo motor with an encoder**, the PLC commands a corresponding reduction in pulse count. **Conclusion:** The PLC must *reduce* the dosator stroke length by 1.57 mm to maintain the 200 mg target after the powder becomes denser. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the **rotary die process** for soft gelatin capsules with a labeled diagram. Identify the role of the **thermocouple**, **wedge temperature controller**, and **synchronization encoder** in the instrumentation loop. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **rotary die process** is the standard industrial method for manufacturing soft gelatin capsules (SGCs). It operates as follows: 1. Two continuous ribbons of gelatin (from a spreader box) are fed between two counter-rotating die cylinders. 2. The dies contain matched cavities that, as they rotate, press the two ribbons together. 3. Simultaneously, a precision **filling pump** injects the liquid formulation through a **wedge** between the ribbons at the moment of die engagement. 4. The combination of mechanical pressure (die cavities) and **thermal sealing** (the wedge is heated to 35–45 °C) hermetically seals the capsule. 5. The capsule is then trimmed from the ribbon, washed, dried, and inspected. **Instrumentation Loop:** - **Thermocouple (J-type):** Mounted at the die wedge. Its millivolt output is linearized and transmitted as a 4–20 mA signal to a **PID temperature controller**. The controller modulates a **heater cartridge** in the wedge to maintain the gelatin at its sealing temperature (above gel point but below degradation, ≈ 40 °C nominal). - **Wedge Temperature Controller:** Implements PID with anti-windup. Setpoint is set by the operator HMI; alarm limits (e.g., 30 °C low, 50 °C high) trigger SCADA notifications. - **Synchronization Encoder:** Mounted on the die shaft. Provides **pulse train** proportional to die angular position. The PLC uses this to trigger the **filling pump** at the exact moment the die cavity is aligned with the wedge nozzle. This ensures *each* cavity receives the correct dose, synchronized to a precision of ±2°. | Instrument | Sensor | Controller | Actuator | Variable | |---|---|---|---|---| | Die wedge | J-thermocouple | PID TC | Heater cartridge | Wedge T | | Filling pump | Encoder | PLC motion ctrl | Servo pump | Fill volume | | Spreader box | RTD + level probe | PID TC + LCV | Steam CV + pump | Ribbon thickness | | Ribbon tension | Load cell on dancer roll | PLC | Brake tensioner | Web tension | **(b)** A softgel encapsulation line operates at 20,000 capsules/hour. Each capsule receives 500 mg of liquid fill. The checkweigher measures a standard deviation of 8 mg. Calculate the **process capability index** $C_p$ given the specification limits of **±25 mg** around the target. Comment on the process suitability. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - Target fill weight: $\mu = 500$ mg - Standard deviation: $\sigma = 8$ mg - Upper Specification Limit (USL): $\mu + 25 = 525$ mg - Lower Specification Limit (LSL): $\mu - 25 = 475$ mg **Step 1 — Compute $C_p$:** $$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$$ Substituting: $$C_p = \frac{525 - 475}{6 \times 8} = \frac{50}{48} \approx 1.042$$ **Step 2 — Compute $C_{pk}$ (assuming mean is on-target):** $$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$$ $$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{25}{24}, \frac{25}{24}\right) = \frac{25}{24} \approx 1.042$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** - $C_p = 1.042$ is **marginally above 1.0**, meaning the process is *just barely capable* but lacks safety margin. - Industry standard for **pharmaceutical processes** demands $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ for established processes and $C_{pk} \geq 1.67$ for new processes (per FDA Process Validation Guidance). **Step 4 — Recommendations:** 1. **Reduce variability:** Investigate sources of fill-weight variation — pump pulsation, gelatin ribbon tension, temperature drift. 2. **Re-tune the PID controller** on the filling pump using **IMC (Internal Model Control)** tuning rules. 3. **Implement in-line NIR monitoring** for real-time content uniformity. 4. **Apply Statistical Process Control (SPC)** charts (X-bar, R-charts) to monitor drift over time. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > 1. Students often confuse $C_p$ with $C_{pk}$ — they are **equal only if the process mean is exactly at the target**. Always compute $C_{pk}$ separately. > 2. Unit consistency: weight in mg, deviation in mg. Mixing g and mg is a common 1-mark loss. > 3. Interpretation: $C_p \geq 1.33$ is *capable*, but $C_p < 1.0$ is *not capable*. State this explicitly for full marks. > 4. When asked for "process suitability," always tie back to the relevant pharmacopeial standard (USP <905> or ICH Q8). --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Capsule** = solid dosage form enclosed in a gelatin/HPMC shell; comes in *hard* (two-piece, dip-coated) and *soft* (one-piece, rotary die) variants. - The **KTU Module 4** focus is on *instrumentation and control*, not pharmacology. - **Critical unit operations:** gelatin melt → dipping → drying → stripping → filling → checkweighing → sorting → polishing → packaging. - **Core sensors:** PT100 RTD, capacitive RH sensor, load cell, vision camera, J-type thermocouple, incremental encoder. - **Core controllers:** PID temperature controller, PLC with discrete PID, cascade controllers for multi-zone dryers. - **Key formulas:** PID equation (continuous and discrete), $W = \rho V$, $v_{\text{pin}} = L / t$, $C_p = (\text{USL} - \text{LSL}) / 6\sigma$. - **Standards:** USP <905> for fill weight uniformity; ASTM E2503 for capsule dimension standards. - **Closed-loop architecture:** Setpoint → Error → PID → Actuator → Plant → Sensor → Feedback. - **Process capability:** Pharmaceutical industry demands $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$. - **Softgel die temperature** is the most tightly controlled parameter (35–45 °C). - **Industrial speeds:** Hard capsules up to 150,000/hr; softgels up to 30,000/hr. - **Always** map sensor → transmitter → controller → actuator in your answer for full marks on instrumentation questions.
